description: Covers the biomedical applications of data science techniques, which include pre-processing techniques, machine learning data analysis, and data visualization techniques.

title: BME 4882 Senior Design, Professionalism and Ethics 1 3 Credits
description: Design of custom strategies to address real-life issues in the development of biocompatible and biomimetic devices for biotechnology or biomedical applications. Teams work with a client in the development of projects that incorporate various aspects of biomedical engineering including instrumentation, biomechanics, biotransport, tissue engineering and others. Emphasizes formal engineering design principles; overview of intellectual properties, engineering ethics, risk analysis, safety in design and FDA regulations are reviewed. Part 1 focuses on design.

title: BME 4883 Senior Design, Professionalism and Ethics 2 3 Credits
description: Design of custom strategies to address real-life issues in the development of biocompatible and biomimetic devices for biotechnology or biomedical applications. Teams work with a client in the development of projects that incorporate various aspects of biomedical engineering including instrumentation, biomechanics, biotransport, tissue engineering and others. Emphasizes formal engineering design principles; overview of intellectual properties, engineering ethics, risk analysis, safety in design and FDA regulations are reviewed. Part 2 focuses on implementation and testing.

title: BME 4931 Special Topics in Biomedical Engineering 1-4 Credits
description: Selected topics in biomedical engineering.

title: EGN 1935 Special Topics in Freshman Engineering 1-3 Credits
description: Laboratory, lectures or conferences cover selected topics in engineering.

title: EGN 4912 Engineering Directed Independent Research 0-3 Credits
description: Provides firsthand, supervised research with a faculty advisor or postdoctoral or graduate student mentor. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery, or application.

title: BCH 3023 Elementary Organic and Biological Chemistry 3 Credits
description: Elementary organic chemistry and biochemistry for students in the agricultural technical curricula. This is a terminal course and is not part of any sequence.

title: BOT 2010C Introductory Botany 3 Credits
description: Intro Botany, for science majors. Introductory-entry level course primarily for beginning students or new college students. First course in botany. Structures  functions of cells, tissues, and organs of flowering plants. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.

title: BOT 2011C Plant Diversity 4 Credits
description: Survey of major plant groups with regard to structure, life histories and uses accompanied by laboratory showing diversity of plants in the world. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.

title: BOT 2710C Practical Plant Taxonomy 3 Credits
description: Introduces plant taxonomy including principles of systematic botany, nomenclature and classification, but emphasizing identification. Student will be able to identify the common ferns, fern allies, gymnosperms and flowering plants of field and garden.

title: BOT 2800C Plants in Human Affairs 3 Credits
description: Role of plants in development of civilization and influence of plants on world history, politics, economics and culture. Survey of useful and harmful plants and plant products. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.

title: BOT 3151C Local Flora of North Florida 3 Credits
description: Laboratory observation of the gross features of vascular plants and practice in the use of keys to identify plants. Elementary ecology of principal types of plant communities in northern Florida. Includes field trips.

title: BOT 3503 Physiology and Molecular Biology of Plants 3 Credits
description: The chemical organization, cellular organization, metabolism, nutrition, growth and molecular biology of the higher plants.

title: BOT 3503L Physiology and Molecular Biology of Plants Laboratory 2 Credits
description: Laboratory experiments to accompany BOT 3503.

title: BOT 4053 Practical Experience in Teaching Botany 2 Credits
description: Participation in teaching one 3000-level botany course with practical experience in instructional procedures, testing and grading, course and laboratory preparation and laboratory assistance.

title: BOT 4650 Plant Symbiosis 3 Credits
description: Examines the crucial role of symbioses in shaping the diversity of life. Topics include generalities among symbioses, origins and establishment of symbioses, and coevolution and cospeciation, as well as specifics of well-studied exemplars of bacterial, fungal, animal, and plant symbioses with plants.

title: BOT 4905 Individual Studies in Botany 2-4 Credits
description: Qualified students and an instructor choose a particular problem for study.

title: BOT 4911 Undergraduate Research in Botany 0-3 Credits
description: Provides firsthand, supervised research in Botany. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery or application in Botany.

title: BOT 4935 Special Topics 1-4 Credits
description: Special topics in botany.

title: BOT 4956 Overseas Studies 1-15 Credits
description: Overseas Studies

title: PCB 3023 Essential Cell Biology 3 Credits
description: Introduces the basic concepts of molecular cell biology in prokaryotic and eukaryotic systems including experimental strategies and methodology. This course is intended for those interested in plants.

title: PCB 3601C Plant Ecology 3 Credits
description: Principles of ecology at scales ranging from individual plants to landscapes. Emphasis is on species, ecosystems and environmental programs in Florida.

title: GEB 2015 Business and You: Warrington Welcome 1 Credit
description: Introduces students to the college of business by focusing on various resources and strategies for success, including personal and professional development opportunities that enhance career preparedness.

title: GEB 2030 Exploring Careers in Business 1 Credit
description: Overview of the various career paths available through the business majors available at UF; focuses on exploration of the sub-sectors within business and will help students with career discernment.

title: GEB 2041 Career Accelerator 1 Credit
description: Prepares students to secure internships and full-time jobs through emphasis on career readiness competencies. Covers assessing career goals, crafting effective resumes, excelling in interviews, and mastering networking. Learn to identify and put into practice various skills through group work with fellow classmates. Also emphasizes self-reflection, exploration, and teamwork, enabling students to make informed decisions about their future career paths.

title: GEB 2210 Advanced Presentation Skills and Principles of Case Analysis 1 Credit
description: Teaches advanced presentation skills to prepare for case competitions and to excel when delivering professional presentations.

title: GEB 2216 Professional Writing in Business 3 Credits
description: Designed to teach students fundamental written communication skills focusing on those areas central to professional writing in the workplace.

title: GEB 2219 Writing and Speaking in Business 4 Credits
description: Writing and speaking in business is an online course designed to teach business students the fundamentals of both written and spoken communication, allowing them to express their ideas effectively and efficiently and also preparing them for the demands of the business workspace.

title: GEB 2951 Special Projects 1-3 Credits
description: Projects related to business as approved by the college.

title: GEB 3017 Leading Organizations 4 Credits
description: Provides a Strategic Foundation for Leadership Practice in a Variety of Professions. Increases the Capacity to Lead With and Without Authority, Across Boundaries, and From Any Political or Organizational Position.

title: GEB 3035 Effective Career Management in Business 4 Credits
description: Systematically designed course based on the cognitive information processing theory for career problem solving and decision-making. Delivered via modularized multimedia instructional materials. The course is designed to inform students about career planning and management interventions and to accommodate students at different levels of decidedness about their career aspirations.

title: GEB 3218 Professional Speaking in Business 3 Credits
description: Teaches fundamental oral communication skills necessary for succeeding in a business setting including presentations and speeches, interpersonal skills and interviewing.

title: GEB 3263 Applied Leadership: Peer Led Facilitation in Business 2 Credits
description: Develops presentation, communication, and leadership skills through self-discovery, community building, classroom management, and facilitation techniques utilizing the framework of servant leadership.

title: GEB 3373 International Business 4 Credits
description: Exposes the business environments (culture, politics, laws and economics) encountered in other parts of the world; to the tools and skills necessary to analyze the potential of other countries as locations for production, distribution and marketing; to how managing and integrating international operations in many parts of the world differs from the domestic focus of many business courses; and to the broad perspective required of successful managers working in international business.

title: GEB 3523 Business Case Analysis 2 Credits
description: Introduces case analysis and prepares students for national and international case competitions.

title: GEB 3931 Special Topics in Business Communication 1 Credit
description: Through rotating special topics and content, this course has two distinct goals: to develop sophisticated, adaptable communication skills by tailoring communication to different audiences and to build and maintain strong professional relationships through communication.

title: GEB 3942 Business Externship: Micro-Consulting 1 Credit
description: Students in this course apply relevant business skills and knowledge to help a company complete a specified and detailed project. Acting as consultants to the company, students are put in project or consulting teams and work together on the project while communicating with, and getting guidance by, a company contact. While applying information obtained in other business courses, students taking this course will focus on professional development skills and other important business skills.

title: GEB 4900 Leadership Launch 2 Credits
description: Capstone course provides a foundation in effective leadership practice grounded in theory and research. Students build upon leadership knowledge and experiences, explore key attributes and behaviors, and develop competencies aligned with organizational and business success.

title: GEB 4905 Individual Work 1-4 Credits
description: Reading and/or research in business administration.

title: GEB 4906 Independent Study 1-4 Credits
description: Reading and research in business administration.

title: GEB 4911 Undergraduate Research in Business 0-4 Credits
description: Provides an opportunity for firsthand, supervised research in business. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery, or application in business.

title: GEB 4930 Special Topics 1-4 Credits
description: Variable content provides an opportunity for in-depth study of topics not offered in other courses and topics of special current significance.

title: GEB 4932 Professional Development Module 2 Credits
description: Presents techniques for outstanding presentation and public speaking skills. Includes a significant service learning component whereby students apply presentation techniques developed in the classroom via business-oriented instruction in the K-12 school system.

title: GEB 4941 Internship in Business Administration 1-4 Credits
description: Applied work in business administration that requires several papers and reports.

title: GEB 4956 International Studies in Business 1-18 Credits
description: Provides a mechanism by which coursework taken as part of an approved study abroad program can be recorded on the UF transcript and counted toward graduation.

title: GEB 4970 Honors Thesis 1 Credit
description: A thesis is required for the awarding of the magna cum laude or summa cum laude designation. To qualify for the thesis option, students will normally have completed 90 semester credits of coursework (exceptions may be made by the honors coordinator of the student's major department) and must have at least the 3.6 grade point average required for magna cum laude designation at the time they enroll. The thesis will be reviewed by at least one faculty member chosen by the honors coordinator from the student's major department.

title: SLS 1501 College Study Skills 1 1-3 Credits
description: A developmental course designed to assist students who may be having difficulty in their college-level coursework or those who simply wish to improve basic skills essential for college success. The course begins with an individual self-assessment followed by topics such as motivation, time management, active reading, memory, note taking and test taking. Students will also develop a career portfolio for their final lesson.

title: SLS 2301 Career Planning 1 Credit
SLS 2302 Job Strategies 1 Credit
description: Interactive course covering all aspects of a successful job search, long-term career planning and success in the professional environment. Enrollment is restricted to juniors and seniors.

title: BME 2402 Introduction to Biomolecular Engineering 3 Credits
description: Introduces bio-related careers in chemical engineering, providing foundational knowledge in molecular cell biology and biochemistry for early undergraduate students. Highlights the connections between biology and chemical engineering, helping students understand interdisciplinary concepts that are essential for biomolecular engineering

title: BME 4220 Biomolecular Cell Mechanics 3 Credits
description: Covers the biomolecular basis of cell mechanics and cell motility, emphasizing quantitative models and systems-biology approaches.

title: BME 4321 Dynamics of Cellular Processes 3 Credits
description: Confocal florescence microscopy, techniques for imaging macromolecular dynamics and interactions inside living cells, models of intracellular diffusion, models of ligand-receptor binding, interplay between binding and transport, modeling and analysis of experiments. Examples from literature include mRNA transport, nuclear pore dynamics, cytoskeletal dynamics, imaging motor proteins and transcription factor dynamics.

title: COT 3502 Computational Methods for Chemical Engineers 3 Credits
description: Solutions of scientific and engineering problems using digital computers. Formulation of models for describing physical processes, numerical analysis, and computer programming.

title: ECH 2934 Professional Development of Chemical Engineers 1 Credit
description: Introduces the Chemical Engineering profession and a wide range of careers, including chemical, energy, pharmaceutical, semiconductor, and food industries, as well as graduate and professional school pathways. Provides resources to build successful professional careers, including best practices for interviewing, oral presentations, and teamwork. Also discusses professional codes of conduct and ethical decision-making.

title: ECH 3023 Material and Energy Balances 4 Credits
description: Formulation and solution of material and energy balances utilizing physical/chemical properties of matter as applied to analyzing unit operations systems.

title: ECH 3101 Process Thermodynamics 4 Credits
description: Introduces fundamental principles of classical and statistical thermodynamics. Application of the first and second laws of thermodynamics to the modeling and analysis of physical and chemical processes undergoing change, such as liquefaction, refrigeration, and power generation.

title: ECH 3203 Fluid and Solid Operations 3 Credits
description: Characteristics of laminar and turbulent flow, mechanical energy balance, flow through packed beds and fluidization of solids, design of pumping systems and piping networks and metering of fluids.

title: ECH 3223 Energy Transfer Operations 3 Credits
description: Steady state conduction in solids and heterogeneous materials, transient conduction, convection heat transfer, heat transfer during boiling and condensation, radiation heat transfer, design of heat-transfer equipment and heat exchange networks.

title: ECH 3264 Elementary Transport Phenomena 2 Credits
description: Flux law and conservation equations of mass, energy, and momentum; solution of transport problems associated with physical and chemical processing; macroscopic and microscopic analysis.

title: ECH 4123 Phase and Chemical Equilibria 3 Credits
description: Application of thermodynamic principles to systems of variable composition, including the study of phase and chemical equilibria.

title: ECH 4224L Fluid and Energy Transfer Operations Laboratory 2 Credits
description: Laboratory work in unit operations involving heat and momentum transfer.

title: ECH 4323 Process Control Theory 3 Credits
description: Analysis and automatic control of process systems in chemical engineering.

title: ECH 4323L Process Control Laboratory 1 Credit
description: Laboratory work associated with ECH 4323.

title: ECH 4403 Separation and Mass Transfer Operations 3 Credits
description: Theory, design, and evaluation of diffusional and staged mass transfer processes including distillation, absorption and extraction, leaching, and membrane separations. Computer-aided design methods.

title: ECH 4404L Separation and Mass Transfer Operations Laboratory 2 Credits
description: Laboratory work in unit operations involving mass transfer. (WR)

title: ECH 4504 Chemical Kinetics and Reactor Design 4 Credits
description: Homogeneous and heterogeneous reaction kinetic modeling and data analysis. Analysis and design of ideal batch, mixed, plug and recycle reactors. Heterogeneous catalysis and reactor design.

title: ECH 4524 Heterogeneous Chemical Kinetics Reactor Design 2 Credits
description: Theories of catalytic reactions of adsorbed species at solid surfaces, development or rate expressions with heat and mass transport properties through porous catalyst materials for design of heterogeneous chemical reactors.

title: ECH 4604 Process Economics and Optimization 3 Credits
description: Introduces the principles of process economics including specifications and costing of equipment, operations costing and economic evaluation of processes.

title: ECH 4644 Process Design 3 Credits
description: Preliminary design of convention chemical processes including process specifications, sitting and layout, equipment sizing, utility and manpower needs, safety and hazard analysis, environmental considerations and economic evaluation. Planning techniques for detailed engineering, construction and startup.

title: ECH 4714 Chemical Process Safety 3 Credits
description: Laboratory and process safety analysis which emphasizes prevention and mitigation. Application of chemical engineering principles to assessing hazards and risk.

title: ECH 4742 Bioprocess Engineering and Bioseparations 3 Credits
description: Key concepts of bioprocess engineering and bioseparations needed for chemical engineers to succeed in the pharmaceutical industry, the food/beverage industry, and bio-based processes for energy production. Topics include enzymes and enzyme kinetics, growth rates, fermentation, batch processing and batch reactors, and bioactive molecule separation (filtration, gel electrophoresis, chromatography, and other forms of bioseparations for cell, protein, gene, antibody, and viral therapies).

title: ECH 4744 Pharmaceutical Bioengineering 3 Credits
description: Introduces concepts, challenges, and technologies in the modern pharmaceutical industry and laboratory, focused on biologics (drugs derived from living organisms).

title: ECH 4824 Materials of Chemical Engineering 2 Credits
description: Relations between microscopic structure and macroscopic mechanical, thermal and electrical properties of organic and inorganic solids. Engineering applications, including corrosion.

title: ECH 4827 Processing of Complex Fluids 3 Credits
description: Principles involved in quantitative adoption of chemical engineering unit operations and unit processes for the analysis and design of systems involving complex fluids.

title: ECH 4905 Special Problems in Chemical Engineering 1-6 Credits
description: Study of chemical engineering problems as identified by the student and instructor.

title: ECH 4912 Integrated Product and Process Design 1 3 Credits
description: The first part of a two-course sequence in which multidisciplinary teams of engineering and business students partner with industry sponsors to design and build authentic product and processes, on time and within budget. Working closely with industry liaison engineers and a faculty coach, students gain practical experience in teamwork and communication, problem solving and engineering design, and develop leadership, management and people skills.

title: ECH 4913 Integrated Product and Process Design 2 3 Credits
description: The second part of a two-course sequence in which multidisciplinary teams of engineering and business students partner with industry sponsors to design and build authentic product and processes, on time and within budget. Working closely with industry liaison engineers and a faculty coach, students gain practical experience in teamwork and communication, problem solving and engineering design, and develop leadership, management and people skills.

title: ECH 4944 Practical Work in Chemical Engineering 1-5 Credits
description: One term industrial employment, including extra work according a pre-approved outline. Practical engineering work under industrial supervision as set forth in college regulations.

title: ECH 4948 Internship Work Experience 0-3 Credits
description: Practical internship work experience under approved industrial supervision, as set forth in college regulations. A maximum of 3 credits from ECH 4948 and ECH 4949 can count towards the Chemical Engineering degree. For example, a student who has earned 1 credit of ECH 4949, can only have 2 credits of ECH 4948 count towards the degree.

title: ECH 4949 Co-op Work Experience 0-3 Credits
description: Practical co-op work experience under approved industrial supervision, as set forth in college regulations. A maximum of 3 credits from ECH 4949 and ECH 4948 can count towards the Chemical Engineering degree. For example, a student who has earned 1 credit of ECH 4948, can only have 2 credits of ECH 4949 count towards the degree.

title: EGN 1935 Special Topics in Freshman Engineering 1-3 Credits
description: Laboratory, lectures or conferences cover selected topics in engineering.

title: EGN 4912 Engineering Directed Independent Research 0-3 Credits
description: Provides firsthand, supervised research with a faculty advisor or postdoctoral or graduate student mentor. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery, or application.

title: CHM 1020 Chemistry for the Liberal Arts 3 Credits
description: This course provides students with an introduction to chemical principles and applications for the non-science major. Students will engage in problem solving and critical thinking while applying chemical concepts. Topics will include the scientific method of problem solving, classification of matter, atomic theory, the periodic table, gases, chemical reactions, energy, and chemical bonds.

title: CHM 1025 Introduction to Chemistry 2 Credits
description: Introductory readiness course in general chemistry for those with weak but satisfactory backgrounds in high school chemistry and algebra. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.

title: CHM 1030 Basic Chemistry Concepts and Applications 1 3 Credits
description: Terminal sequence for non-science students that presents chemistry from medical/nursing perspective. Topics in inorganic chemistry and properties of both ionic and covalent compounds. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.

title: CHM 1031 Basic Chemistry Concepts and Applications 2 3 Credits
description: Second half of CHM 1030/1031 sequence. Topics in organic chemistry and biochemistry. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.

title: CHM 2045 General Chemistry 1 3 Credits
description: This course is designed for students pursuing careers in the sciences or who need a more rigorous presentation of chemical concepts than is offered in an introductory course. Students will engage in problem solving and critical thinking while applying chemical concepts. Topics will include the principles of chemistry including atomic theory, electronic and molecular structure, measurement, stoichiometry, bonding, periodicity, thermochemistry, nomenclature, solutions, and the properties of gases.

title: CHM 2045L General Chemistry Laboratory 1 Credit
description: Laboratory experiments designed to reflect the topics that are presented in CHM 2045. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.

title: CHM 2046 General Chemistry 2 3 Credits
description: Second semester of the CHM 2045-2045l-2046-2046l sequence. Students who completed 2045 (or equivalent) at another institution should consult with a chemistry academic advisor before registering for this course. Acids and bases, additional aspects of chemical equilibria, thermodynamics, electrochemistry, complex ions and descriptive chemistry. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.

title: CHM 2046L General Chemistry 2 Laboratory 1 Credit
description: Laboratory experiments designed to reflect the topics that are presented in CHM 2046. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.

title: CHM 2047 One-Semester General Chemistry 4 Credits
description: Designed for entering (not transfer) students who wish to move more quickly into advanced coursework. Electronic structure and bonding, gases, liquids, solids, kinetics, equilibria, acids and bases, thermodynamics, oxidation-reduction, metals and non-metals.

title: CHM 2047L One-Semester General Chemistry Laboratory 1 Credit
description: Laboratory experiments designed to accompany CHM 2047.

title: CHM 2050 Honors General Chemistry 1 for Majors 3 Credits
description: First semester of the CHM 2050/2045L and CHM 2051/2046L sequence. Stoichiometry, atomic and molecular structure, the states of matter, reaction rates and equilibria.

title: CHM 2051 Honors General Chemistry 2 3 Credits
description: Second semester of the sequence CHM 2045-2045l-2051-2046l. Course is open to students with superior performance in CHM 2045 or its equivalent. Acids and bases, additional aspects of chemical equilibria, thermodynamics, electrochemistry, complex ions and descriptive chemistry and instructor-chosen topics. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.

title: CHM 2054L Accelerated General Chemistry Laboratory 2 Credits
description: A cross-disciplinary, inquiry-based curriculum that focuses on major themes and concepts in chemistry with an emphasis on their application in modern, quantitative life sciences research. Equivalent to CHM 2045L and CHM 2046L or CHM 2047L.

title: CHM 2095 Applied General Chemistry 1 3 Credits
description: First of the CHM 2095/CHM 2096 sequence. Topics include stoichiometry, energy and thermodynamics, atomic and molecular structure, the states of matter, reaction rates, and introduces chemical equilibria. All topics are taught in an engineering case-study context.

title: CHM 2095L Applied General Chemistry 1 Lab 1 Credit
description: Laboratory experiments designed to complement CHM 2095.

title: CHM 2096 Applied General Chemistry 2 3 Credits
description: This course constitutes the second semester of the two-term sequence of Chemistry I  II. As both a general education requirement and major’s course, CHM2096 serves to teach the scientific method, skills for problem solving, general chemistry knowledge, and a connection to the principles that govern the natural world. Students will acquire knowledge of chemical equilibria, acid-base and solubility equilibria, entropy, free energy, electrochemical devices, solution dynamics, and descriptive inorganic and organic chemistry; all are taught in an engineering case-study context. Students participate in class discussions throughout the semester to reflect on pertinent topics and apply mathematical knowledge and reasoning to solve chemical problems. This course affords students the ability to critically examine and evaluate the principles of the scientific method, model construction, and use the scientific method to explain natural experiences and phenomena.

title: CHM 2096L Applied General Chemistry 2 Lab 1 Credit
description: Laboratory experiments designed to complement CHM 2096.

title: CHM 2200 Fundamentals of Organic Chemistry 3 Credits
description: An elementary course embracing the more important aspects of organic chemistry. Intended for students in programs requiring only one semester of organic chemistry. Not intended for pre-med, pre-dentistry or pre-vet students.

title: CHM 2200L Fundamentals of Organic Chemistry Laboratory 1 Credit
description: Organic laboratory experiments to accompany CHM 2200.

title: CHM 2210 Organic Chemistry 1 3 Credits
description: The first half of the CHM 2210/CHM 2211 sequence intended for majors and preprofessional students. A study of the structures, syntheses and reactions of organic compounds.

title: CHM 2211 Organic Chemistry 2 3 Credits
description: The second half of the CHM 2210/CHM 2211 sequence intended for majors and preprofessional students. A study of the structures, syntheses and reactions of organic compounds.

title: CHM 2211L Organic Chemistry Laboratory 2 Credits
description: Organic laboratory experiments designed to accompany CHM 2210/CHM 2211 or CHM 3217/CHM 3218.

title: CHM 2212 Organic Chemistry 1 for Majors 3 Credits
description: First half of the CHM 2212/CHM 2213 sequence for chemistry majors. A study of structures,  synthesis, and reactions of organic compounds,  with emphasis on mechanism and spectroscopy.

title: CHM 2213 Organic Chemistry 2 for Majors 3 Credits
description: Second half of the CHM 2212/CHM 2213 sequence is for chemistry majors. Study of structures,  synthesis, and reactions of organic compounds, with emphasis on mechanism and spectroscopy.

title: CHM 3100 Introduction to Analytical Chemistry for Majors 3 Credits
description: Introduces the basics of analytical chemistry and their use to make quantitative measurements. Examines both classical and modern techniques with a greater focus on modern methods and recent developments. Discusses statistical analyses, data interpretation, and some quantitative analysis.  Designed for traditional CHM majors, excluding the BioChem track.

title: CHM 3120 Introduction to Analytical Chemistry 3 Credits
description: Principles involved in quantitative analysis. Topics include acid-base equilibria and titrations, precipitation and complex formation, oxidation reduction and statistical treatment of data. Introduces spectrochemical and electrochemical methods of analysis and chemical separations.

title: CHM 3120L Analytical Chemistry Laboratory 1 Credit
description: Laboratory experiments designed to accompany CHM 3120.

title: CHM 3217 Organic Chemistry/Biochemistry 1 4 Credits
description: A rigorous, one-semester overview of the structure, properties and reactions of organic compounds. This is the first half of a two-semester sequence in biochemistry. Students are expected to take CHM 3218 after completing CHM 3217.

title: CHM 3218 Organic Chemistry/Biochemistry 2 4 Credits
description: Introduces the basic concepts of biochemistry and molecular biology from the structural and mechanistic perspective of organic chemistry.

title: CHM 3400 Physical Chemistry for the Biosciences 3 Credits
description: Thermodynamics, electrochemistry, transport, chemical kinetics and molecular structure with emphasis on biological systems.

title: CHM 3610 Inorganic Chemistry 3 Credits
description: Basic theoretical concepts involved in inorganic chemistry. Periodic trends, chemical bonding, structure and reactivity.

title: CHM 3610L Inorganic Chemistry Laboratory 2 Credits
description: Synthesis and characterization of inorganic and organometallic compounds.

title: CHM 4034 Advanced Biochemistry and Chemical Biology 3 Credits
description: Primary and secondary metabolism and biosynthetic pathways, protein folding and trafficking, cellular signaling, replication and translation from a chemist's perspective. Applications and primary literature in bioanalytical chemistry and in molecular and cell biology.

title: CHM 4130 Instrumental Analysis 3 Credits
description: Concepts of instrumentation for chemical analysis. In-depth examination of spectrochemical and electrochemical methods and chemical separations.

title: CHM 4130L Instrumental Analysis Laboratory 2 Credits
description: Laboratory experiments designed to accompany CHM 4130.

title: CHM 4143C Electronics and Instrumentation 3 Credits
description: Principles of operation of instrumentation, optimization of instrumental conditions, and interpretation of instrumental data for qualitative and quantitative analysis. Application of electronic principles necessary to code for automated electronic measurements in chemical research.

title: CHM 4230 Organic Spectroscopy 2 Credits
description: Characterization and identification of organic compounds by spectral methods including IR, UV, NMR and mass spectrometry.

title: CHM 4272 The Organic Chemistry of Polymers 2 Credits
description: Classification of polymerization types and mechanisms from a mechanistic/organic point of view. The structure of synthetic and natural polymers and polyelectrolytes. Reactions of polymers and practical synthetic methods of polymer preparation.

title: CHM 4300L Laboratory in Biochemistry and Molecular Biology 2 Credits
description: Introduces experimental techniques in biochemistry, especially those used in DNA isolation and manipulation, protein purification and enzyme kinetic studies.

title: CHM 4304 Chemical Aspects of Cellular Control 3 Credits
description: Control of information, materials and energy within cellular systems. Examples taken from the biochemistry of plants, bacteria and higher organisms.

title: CHM 4308 Introduction to Enzyme Mechanism 3 Credits
description: Principles of enzyme structure, isolation and purification and principles of the physical chemistry of enzyme/substrate interactions. Overview of concepts of biological catalysis, including transition state theory, descriptions and examples of mechanisms of biochemical catalysis, survey of co-factors, and catalytic antibodies, ribozyme structure and catalysis.

title: CHM 4411 Physical Chemistry: Thermodynamics and Kinetics 4 Credits
description: Gas laws, kinetic theory, classical and statistical thermodynamics and applications to solutions, phase equilibria, chemical equilibria and electrochemistry.

title: CHM 4411L Physical Chemistry Laboratory 2 Credits
description: A series of laboratory experiments designed to accompany CHM 4411.

title: CHM 4412 Physical Chemistry: Chemical Bonding and Spectroscopy 4 Credits
description: Introduces quantum theory, atomic and molecular structure, chemical bonding and spectra, chemical reaction rate laws and mechanisms, and statistical and collision theories of reaction rates.

title: CHM 4412L Physical Chemistry Laboratory 2 Credits
description: A series of laboratory experiments designed to accompany CHM 4412.

title: CHM 4413L Biophysical Chemistry Laboratory 2 Credits
description: Laboratory experiments to demonstrate basic concepts of the physical chemistry of biological systems.

title: CHM 4611 Advanced Inorganic Chemistry 3 Credits
description: Introduces advanced concepts of modern inorganic chemistry. Major themes include application of group theory to structure and bonding, molecular orbital treatment of reactivity, fundamental organometallic chemistry and introduces vibrational and electronic spectroscopy.

title: CHM 4671 Bioinorganic Chemistry 3 Credits
description: From an inorganic perspective, introduces the structure and function of a variety of metalloproteins and metalloenzymes, concentrating on systems containing transition metals. Emphasizes the role that metal ion(s) play in the function of the biomolecules.

title: CHM 4905 Individual Problems 1-3 Credits
description: Double registration permitted. An assigned reading program or development of an assigned experimental problem.

title: CHM 4910 Undergraduate Research 0-3 Credits
description: Laboratory or literature investigations of chemical problems of current interest.

title: CHM 4930 Special Topics 1-4 Credits
description: Special topics in chemistry.

title: CHM 4940 Supervised Teaching 0-2 Credits
description: Supervised teaching, usually at the general chemistry level. Required is a superior record at UF in the course in which the student will assist and evidence that the student's own progress will not be delayed.

title: CHM 4956 Overseas Studies 1-15 Credits
IDH 3931 Interdisciplinary Junior Honors 1-3 Credits
description: Special topics restricted to those in the university-wide honors program.

title: IDS 2334 Chemistry in the Cocina Latina 3 Credits
description: Cross-disciplinary exploration of the intersection between the science of chemistry and the culture of the Spanish-speaking world. Studies the chemical reactions inherent in cooking while considering the role of food in the development and maintenance of culture. Active learning in laboratory and kitchen settings enhance the course content.

title: ISC 1010C Secrets of Alchemy 3 Credits
description: Multi-disciplinary exploration of the history of alchemy, the precursor to modern chemistry. Studies the multi-dimensional relationships with philosophy, religion, and the natural sciences from antiquity to the modern era. Incorporates perspectives from western tradition. Recreation of alchemical recipes in the teaching lab enhance the course content.

title: ISC 2400L Cross-Disciplinary Laboratory 1 3 Credits
description: First course in a two-semester inquiry-based laboratory focusing on major themes and concepts in biology, chemistry and physics with an emphasis on their integrated applications in modern, quantitative research. Satisfies course requirements for BSC 2010L, CHM 2045L and PHY 2053L.

title: ISC 2401L Cross-Disciplinary Laboratory 2 3 Credits
description: Second course in a two-semester inquiry-based laboratory focusing on major themes and concepts in biology, chemistry and physics with an emphasis on their integrated applications in modern, quantitative research. Satisfies course requirements for BSC 2011L, CHM 2046L and PHY 2054L.

title: ISC 3523C Research Methods 3 Credits
description: The tools scientists use to solve scientific problems, including use of experiments to answer scientific questions, design of experiments to reduce systematic and random errors, use of statistics to interpret experimental results and deal with sampling errors, mathematical modeling of scientific phenomena and oral presentation of scientific work.

title: CHI 1130 Beginning Chinese 1 5 Credits
description: Beginning study covering four skills: listening, speaking, reading and writing. Materials are designed for learners with no prior exposure to the language. Those with native background or education for four years or more in a Chinese speaking country must take a placement test before enrolling in any Chinese language class.

title: CHI 1131 Beginning Chinese 2 5 Credits
description: Continued study of the four skills with additional vocabulary and grammar.

title: CHI 2230 Intermediate Chinese 1 5 Credits
description: Intermediate study of the four skills with new vocabulary and grammar.

title: CHI 2231 Intermediate Chinese 2 5 Credits
description: Continuation of intermediate study of the four skills with new vocabulary and grammar.

title: CHI 2340 Chinese for Heritage Learners 1 4 Credits
description: For those with significant bilingual speaking and listening backgrounds. Emphasis is on recognition of the characters and writing, and pronunciation and speaking.

title: CHI 2341 Chinese for Heritage Learners 2 4 Credits
description: To consolidate the foundation built in Chinese for Heritage Learners 1, to expand vocabulary and to introduce more complex grammatical structures. Emphasis is on reading and writing. Those who successfully complete CHI 2341 are eligible for CHI 3410.

title: CHI 3403 Chinese Calligraphy 3 Credits
description: Introductory study of the origin, composition, development, variations and aesthetic styles of Chinese characters with laboratory sessions for appreciating and practicing calligraphic skills. (N)

title: CHI 3410 Advanced Chinese 1 3 Credits
description: Advanced study of the four skills with attention to more complex structures.

title: CHI 3411 Advanced Chinese 2 3 Credits
description: Continuation of advanced study of the four skills with attention to more complex structures.

title: CHI 3440 Business Chinese 3 Credits
description: Development of language skills and protocol issues used in Chinese business environments. Acquire vocabulary, phrases, and sentence patterns essential for business transactions and develop oral presentations, business cards, and resumes.

title: CHI 4050 Fourth Year Chinese 1 3 Credits
description: Introduces and analyzes the documentary prose style used in Chinese newspapers and media. By studying a variety of short texts students develop skill in listening, speaking, reading and writing about issues commonly encountered in Chinese newspaper editorials and in television news programs, debate roundtables, or TV documentaries.

title: CHI 4051 Fourth Year Chinese 2 3 Credits
description: Solidifies and improves students' knowledge of advanced Chinese through literature as a continuation of CHI 4050. Emphasizes formulating strategies to learn the difference between written language and spoken language and approaches meaningful writing with an eye toward careful examination of authentic writing samples.

title: CHI 4850 Structure of Chinese 3 Credits
description: Introduction to phonological, grammatical and discourse structures of Mandarin Chinese, with an emphasis on its contrastive aspects with the English language. (N)

title: CHI 4905 Individual Study 1-5 Credits
description: For those who seek independent work not offered in another course. Includes all individual study courses offered by the Chinese section.

title: CHI 4911 Undergraduate Research in Language or Linguistics 0-3 Credits
description: Provides firsthand, supervised research in Language or Linguistics. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery or application in Language or Linguistics.

title: CHI 4930 Special Topics in Chinese Studies 3 Credits
description: Variable topics dealing with specific issues in Chinese studies.

title: CHI 4935 Senior Thesis 3 Credits
description: Select a Chinese faculty member to act as director for an independent research project that culminates in the preparation of an honors thesis.

title: CHI 4940 Internship 1-6 Credits
description: Gain practical experience that enhances classroom learning.

title: CHI 4956 Overseas Studies 1-18 Credits
CHT 3101 Translation Method: Chinese to English 3 Credits
description: Hands-on introduction to the processes, procedures, and cultural and linguistic challenges relevant to translation from Chinese to English. Development of basic translation skills including strategies on word level, sentence level, and text level. Acquisition of lexicon, structure, and style used in various fields (legal, business, medical) and hands-on experience in translating real-life documents.

title: CHT 3110 Chinese Literary Heritage 3 Credits
description: Introduces pre-modern Chinese literature in translation. Topics chosen from classical poetry, short stories, novels, and drama. Emphasizes the interplay between orthodox values and the folk tradition. All readings are in English.

title: CHT 3123 Pre-Modern Chinese Fiction in Translation 3 Credits
description: Pre-modern Chinese narrative from its philosophical and historical origins to the fiction at the turn of the 20th century. Emphasizes the 16th and 17th centuries when Chinese vernacular fiction flourished.

title: CHT 3124 Modern Chinese Fiction in Translation 3 Credits
description: Surveys modern Chinese fiction in translation. Samples are from the early 20th century through the contemporary era and include writers of the early Republic, the P.R.C., and Taiwan. Focuses on fiction as a vehicle for social change. All readings are in English.

title: CHT 3302 Tales of the Strange in Medieval Chinese Literature 3 Credits
description: Introduces traditional Chinese tales of the strange and primary sources that contributed to their development. Also covers cross-cultural literary theories of the fantastic and uncanny; interdisciplinary literary, discourse, and narrative theories; and three common medieval Chinese worldviews.

title: CHT 3391 Chinese Film and Media 3 Credits
description: Examination of Chinese cinema and other forms of media, such as television, music, and print culture in a broad sociopolitical and historical context. An interdisciplinary approach with a diversity of readings and multimedia tools incorporated into discussions.

title: CHT 3500 Chinese Culture 3 Credits
description: Introduction to Chinese culture with emphasis on its philosophy, language, society, art, and people as a whole. All readings are in English.

title: CHT 3511 Chinese Martial Arts Fiction 3 Credits
description: Overview of Chinese martial arts fiction from earliest times to the present day. Discusses the literary pleasure of watching violence, the relationship between violence and the law, gender ambiguity of woman warriors, the imperial and (trans)national order of martial arts narrative, and the moral and physical economy of vengeance.

title: CHT 3513 Taoism and Chinese Culture 3 Credits
description: Introduction to the general history and culture of Taoism in ancient and modern China: its thoughts, belief systems, cultural influences, practices, and rituals.

title: CHT 3515 Bodies, Bodies, Bodies in Premodern China 3 Credits
description: Surveys a selection of classical approaches to the study of the body before analyzing and comparing several common literary, philosophical, medical (TCM), mantic, religious, and political representations of the human body in different eras of premodern Chinese culture. Tensions between traditionalism and modernity are examined in the final weeks of the course. All readings and discussion are in English.

title: CHT 3523 Hong Kong, Taiwan, and the New Global Cinema 3 Credits
description: Overview of Sinophone film in Hong Kong, Taiwan, and the broader sphere of Chinese diaspora. Addresses film history, culture, and aesthetics.

title: CHT 4111 Dream of the Red Chamber 3 Credits
description: Explores the intellectual and social life of traditional China through the 18th century epic novel, Story of the Stone. Also studies interpretive theories of the novel, both Chinese and Western. All readings are in English.

title: CHT 4603 Journey to the West 3 Credits
description: Exploration of traditional Chinese religious culture, cultural history and literacy expression through a 100 chapter novel known as Journey to the West, or Monkey.

title: CHT 4911 Undergraduate Research in English Translation 0-3 Credits
description: Provides firsthand, supervised research in English Translation. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery or application in English Translation.

title: CHW 4120 Classical Chinese 1 3 Credits
description: Introduction to classical Chinese prose with texts drawn mainly from early histories and philosophical writings (500 BC - AD 100). Emphasis on reading comprehension, grammar analysis and translation.

title: CHW 4121 Classical Chinese 2 3 Credits
description: Continuation of CHW 4120 focusing on classical Chinese prose with texts drawn from early historical and philosophical texts to belles-lettres of the medieval era and later periods. Emphasis on reading comprehension, grammar analysis, and translation.

title: CHW 4130 Readings in Chinese Literature 3 Credits
description: Introduces advanced language students to a sampling of Chinese writers. Materials chosen from classic or modern/contemporary Chinese literature rotated across semesters. All readings in Chinese.

title: CHW 4140 Newspaper Chinese 3 Credits
description: Development of ability to understand and translate the documentary prose style used in Chinese newspapers and academic journals. Introduces literary function words and grammar structures, with comparison to the vernacular. Most readings in the simplified character form used in the PRC; all readings in Chinese. Applications for research on modern China.

title: CHW 4911 Undergraduate Research in Target Language 0-3 Credits
description: Provides firsthand, supervised research. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery or application.

title: CCE 4015 Civil Engineering Estimating 3 Credits
description: Design of systems for estimating and cost control of human-machine productivity for civil engineering projects. Analysis of cost factors required for optimization of engineering-investment efficiency.

title: CCE 4204 Construction Equipment, Methods and Management 3 Credits
description: Theory and practice of construction operations, equipment utilization and construction methods. Analysis of costs. Optimizing crew and equipment. Heavy equipment costs. New emphasis on planning and executing a construction project.

title: CCE 4521C Engineering and Construction Analytics using BIM 3 Credits
description: Exploration of model-based engineering and construction analytics using Building Information Modeling (BIM) to understand structural properties, constructability, and maintainability of structures. Topics include model-based designs; finite element analysis based on BIM; mechanical, electrical and plumbing (MEP) models; constructability and maintainability assessment; model-informed construction time and cost analysis, and reality capture for infrastructure assessment.

title: CCE 4811 Construction Engineering Design 3 Credits
description: Simulation of comprehensive construction project involving all phases of planning, scheduling and control from start to finish, which involves making major decisions. Oral presentation required at the end of course.

title: CEG 4011 Soil Mechanics 4 Credits
description: Physical properties of soils, compaction, flow of water through soil, distribution of stress within soil and consolidation. Laboratory.

title: CEG 4012 Geotechnical Engineering 3 Credits
description: Subsurface exploration settlements analysis, slope stability, earth pressure and an introduction to foundation design.

title: CEG 4104 Retaining Wall and Embankment Design 3 Credits
description: The application of soil mechanics to the design and analysis of various types of retaining structures and earthen embankments.

title: CEG 4111 Foundation Engineering Design 3 Credits
description: Comprehensive design of geotechnical system, focusing on design of complete project and utilizing CAD programs. Designs, drawings and oral presentations through group effort.

title: CES 3102 Mechanics of Engineering Structures 4 Credits
description: Introduces structural load, equilibrium, shear and bending moment diagrams, structural analysis software, classical methods for displacement determination, method of consistent deformations, slope deflection method, moment distribution method.

title: CES 4605 Analysis and Design in Steel 3 Credits
description: Elastic and plastic theories of design, design of members subjected to tension, compression, flexure and torsion. Design of connections and rigid frames.

title: CES 4702 Analysis and Design in Reinforced Concrete 3 Credits
description: Ultimate strength analysis and design of reinforced beams and columns, working stress design for flexure, design of footings and retaining walls.

title: CGN 2002 Introduction to Civil Engineering 1 Credit
description: Introduces the broad field of civil engineering.

title: CGN 3421 Computer Methods in Civil Engineering 3 Credits
description: Review of computer programming. Numerical methods as applied to civil engineering problems and civil engineering software.

title: CGN 3501C Civil Engineering Materials 4 Credits
description: Studies the principal materials used for engineering purposes with special attention to mechanical properties and their importance to the engineer. Hands-on experience in testing of civil engineering materials.

title: CGN 3510 Introduction to Sustainable Engineering 3 Credits
description: Overview of the principles of sustainability as they relate to civil and environmental engineering issues. Discussions and projects facilitate a basic understanding of the production-consumption model and life cycle assessment.

title: CGN 3710 Experimentation and Instrumentation in Civil Engineering 3 Credits
description: Fundamentals and applications of measuring systems commonly used in civil engineering. Topics include recording techniques, strain, force, displacement, flow, temperature, humidity and PH measurements.

title: CGN 4101 Civil Engineering Cost Analysis 3 Credits
description: Analysis of civil engineering proposals, utilizing time-value and related factors. Feasibility and optimum life comparisons. Utility rate derivation, utility/cost method.

title: CGN 4160 Civil Engineering Practice 4 Credits
description: Fundamentals of civil engineering professional practice: project management, construction delivery processes, business concepts, public policy, administration, diversity and equity issues in civil engineering, and leadership.

title: CGN 4304 Machine Learning Applications in Civil Engineering 3 Credits
description: Leverage machine learning state-of-the-art techniques and tools to solve Civil Engineering problems. Apply fundamentals of data analytics and machine learning techniques to real-world tasks and gain essential knowledge and programming skills (using R) in data preprocessing, feature selection, model comparison, hyperparameter tuning, and machine-learning interpretation. Includes case studies and applications for hands-on experience.

title: CGN 4404 Applied Data Science in Civil and Environmental Engineering 3 Credits
description: Introduces the workflows of data science applications, covering the state-of-art techniques in data acquisition, data processing and management, analytics and modeling, and visualization. Critical application of data science concepts and techniques in complex socioeconomic and environmental contexts. Basics of problem formulation and major ethical considerations of applying data science in practice.

title: CGN 4503 Pavement Design 3 Credits
description: Function and material requirements of different elements of flexible and rigid pavement systems; characterization of soils, materials, traffic loads, and environment for design; flexible and rigid pavement design; new developments.

title: CGN 4600 Public Works Engineering and Management Practices 3 Credits
description: Public works profession, organization, administration, and management of operating divisions with emphasis on role of engineer.

title: CGN 4806 Transportation-Water-Materials Design 3 Credits
description: Simulation of a design project experience through the completion and presentation of a comprehensive roadway project design. Students work in multi-disciplinary groups to complete a system design that includes traffic, materials, hydrologic and geotechnical considerations.

title: CGN 4851C Concrete Mixture Design 3 Credits
description: Concrete constituent material types and properties, mixture design for normal strength, high strength, ultra-high performance, self-consolidating, pervious, and fiber-reinforced concrete. Concrete mixture design, testing, and construction practices for durability.

title: CGN 4905 Special Problems in Civil Engineering 1-4 Credits
description: Selected problems or projects in the student's major field of engineering study.

title: CGN 4910 Structures-Geotechnical-Construction Comprehensive System Design 3 Credits
description: Simulation of a design office experience through the completion and presentation of a comprehensive building design. Students work in multi-disciplinary groups to complete a system design that includes structural, geotechnical and construction management considerations.

title: CGN 4949 Co-op Work Experience 1 Credit
description: Co-op work experience in a related field.

title: CWR 3201 Hydrodynamics 4 Credits
description: Classification and properties of fluids, hydrostatics, and conservation of mass, momentum and energy in fluid flow. Potential flow, similitude and physical modeling. Laminar and turbulent pipe flow. Introduces turbomachines.

title: CWR 4202 Hydraulics 3 Credits
description: Fundamental equations for pipe and open conduit flow. Development of design oriented formulas for pipes and open channels. Introduces hydrology.

title: CWR 4306 Urban Stormwater Systems Design 3 Credits
description: Surface-water system design including: time of concentration, peak runoff rate, open-channel flow, gravity storm sewer, culvert, stormwater pumping, filtration systems, hydrograph generation, flood routing, site layout, site grading and permitting.

title: CWR 4542 Water Resources Engineering 3 Credits
description: Study of water resources engineering applications including hydrology and statistics, groundwater, hydraulic machinery, dams and reservoirs, water quality, water quality modeling, water and waste-water treatment and water law and institutions.

title: EGN 4912 Engineering Directed Independent Research 0-3 Credits
description: Provides firsthand, supervised research with a faculty advisor or postdoctoral or graduate student mentor. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery, or application.

title: ENV 4514C Water and Wastewater Treatment 3 Credits
description: Design of water and wastewater treatment units.

title: EOC 4851 Coastal Resilience and Engineering with Nature 3 Credits
description: Introduces concepts of coastal resilience and engineering with nature with a focus on flood risk management. Topics include coastal system behavior, principles and frameworks for engineering with nature, stakeholder engagement, benefits and cost, types of natural and nature-based features, and adaptation. Discusses case studies and recent innovations. A team project applies concepts to a project site.

title: EOC 4852 Coastal Dynamics and Engineering Applications 3 Credits
description: Introduces coastal engineering and coastal processes, highlighting coastal engineering projects. Topics include large-scale coastal behavior, linear wave theory, wave generation and propagation, coastal nearshore hydrodynamics, coastal sediment processes, coastal inlet behavior, and coastal protection measures. Also discusses recent innovations in coastal engineering practice.

title: TTE 4004C Transportation Engineering 4 Credits
description: Overview of the significance of highway transportation to the social and economic underpinnings of society. Introduces road vehicle performance, geometric design of highways, traffic flow and queuing theory, highway capacity and level of service analysis, traffic control and analysis at signalized intersections, and travel demand and traffic forecasting.

title: TTE 4106 Urban Transportation Planning 3 Credits
description: Overview of the four-step urban transportation planning process; includes analytical techniques for estimating future travel demand and state-of-the-art approaches.

title: TTE 4201 Traffic Engineering 3 Credits
description: General review of the fundamentals of traffic engineering with emphasis on field studies and data analysis.

title: TTE 4203 Highway Capacity Analysis 3 Credits
description: Provide students with detailed instruction on the procedures defined within the 2010 Highway Capacity Manual (HCM), including analytical chapters for uninterrupted and interrupted flow.

title: TTE 4300 Transportation Systems Analysis 3 Credits
description: Systems analysis in transportation planning and engineering, including supply, demand, equilibrium, evaluation and decision analysis.

title: TTE 4824 Transportation Facility Design 3 Credits
description: Simulates a comprehensive design of a transportation facility, specifically an arterial-freeway interchange. Utilizes state and national-level design manuals in preparation of standard plans. Applies the theoretical background gained in supporting classes, in areas such as traffic analysis, roadway design, roadway drainage, and pavement design. Some review of this material is provided, as well as introduction of several new concepts. Emphasizes teamwork skills and technical communication skills.

title: CLA 1011 Democracy in Theory and Action 3 Credits
description: The birth and evolution of democracy and its contributions to western civilization and current forms of government and governing. Lively dialogue between ancient democracy and modern through a comparative analysis, tackling some of the most pressing issues, such as limits of political participation and free speech, the role of the press/media, and elitism in politics.

title: CLA 1530 Gardens to Read and Visit 3 Credits
description: Investigation of gardens as cultural artifacts in literature and art to discover the extent to which gardens are sites of comfort as well as contest, through analysis of the tension between the experience (“to visit”) and the representations (“to read”) of gardens in written and visual forms.

title: CLA 1812 Medicine, Science, and the Dawn of Reason 3 Credits
description: This interdisciplinary course invites students to examine the passage of humanity into the era of reason and the development of medical science and ethics. As part of the “nature and culture” theme, it presents a historical narrative on the passage of humanity from superstition to the era of scientific inquiry as represented throughout the Western canon and world literatures.

title: CLA 1820 Magic and the Supernatural: From Greece and Rome to the Present 3 Credits
description: Explores beliefs in magic and the supernatural and how they are shaping our culture, values and legal system. Considers how reason, belief, faith and the supernatural interact with each other our life experiences.

title: CLA 2100 The Glory That Was Greece 3 Credits
description: Exploration of the historical, cultural, and socio-political aspects of the Ancient Greek world as it is situated firmly within the western classical tradition.

title: CLA 2120 The Grandeur That Was Rome 3 Credits
description: Provides a multi-faceted introduction to the culture of Rome and its contribution to western civilization and canon. Primary source material read in translation.

title: CLA 2521 Classical Antiquity and Sustainability 3 Credits
description: Examines classical antiquity, applying methods and theories of sustainability to explore the impact of the ancient Greeks and Romans on their environment and focusing on the distribution of wealth, social stratification, land use and classical representations of the natural world.

title: CLA 3020 Ancient War Commemorations 3 Credits
description: Examination of war depictions in public monuments, commemorations, and memorials of the ancient Mediterranean world from Egypt and the Near East to Greece and Rome.

title: CLA 3111 Athens in Topography and Monuments 3 Credits
description: Explores the topography and monuments of ancient Athens to understand social, historical, economic, and religious institutions.

title: CLA 3114 Greece Today and Yesterday 3 Credits
description: Interdisciplinary examination of various aspects of ancient and modern Greek life and culture.

title: CLA 3122 Rome of Augustus 3 Credits
description: Studies the political, social, and cultural developments of Rome during the age of Augustus.

title: CLA 3151 Pompeii: An Archaeological Laboratory 3 Credits
description: Studies the material remains of a Roman town through an examination of the excavated finds: architecture, wall-paintings, and inscriptions.

title: CLA 3160 Ancient Egypt 3 Credits
description: Studies the civilization, culture, and monuments of ancient Egypt from prehistoric times to the New Kingdom and Late period.

title: CLA 3161 Introduction to Hieroglyphics 3 Credits
description: A beginner's course in Egyptian hieroglyphics of the Middle Kingdom. Also designed for students with no former instruction in ancient languages.

title: CLA 3433 The Athenian Democracy 3 Credits
description: Studies ancient Athens from c.650-322 BCE, with attention to the political, social, and cultural institutions of the democracy, drawing on primary sources and modern theory to explain democratic changes from the archaic to the classical period.

title: CLA 3434 Classical Greece 3 Credits
description: Studies the social, political, and cultural developments of classical Greece from the Persian Wars to the death of Alexander (500-322 BCE).

title: CLA 3500 Sport and Recreation in the Ancient World 3 Credits
description: Examines literary and archaeological sources to determine the social, economic, political and cultural importance of games in the ancient Mediterranean world from the time of Homer to the fall of the Roman Empire.

title: CLA 3504 Gender and Sexuality in Classical Antiquity 3 Credits
description: Explores perceptions of the masculine and feminine in Ancient Greece and Rome, discussing these stereotypes in their political, social, economic, and cultural contexts.

title: CLA 3590 Slavery in the Ancient World 3 Credits
description: Examination of slavery in the ancient Greek and Roman world from the late Bronze Age to the end of the Roman empire.

title: CLA 3700 Classical Archaeology 3 Credits
description: Illustrated lectures on archaeology, its process of discovery, and methods. History of major archaeological exploration in Mediterranean lands compared with extant literary information.

title: CLA 3791 The Ancient City: Greek Cities 3 Credits
description: Covers the development of the cities of classical Greece from Minoan centers through Byzantium. All aspects of city life: design, building techniques, culture, and ideas.

title: CLA 3793 The Ancient City: Roman Cities 3 Credits
description: Studies the cities of the Roman Empire from the founding of Rome to the establishment of Constantinople as the Eastern capital. Emphasizes life in imperial Rome and in European and North African cities.

title: CLA 3811 AI in Antiquity and Today 3 Credits
description: Examines AI’s origins in ancient Greece and compares it to AI’s acceptance and use in modern society. Pairs discussion of Greek and Roman philosophical and other literary texts on the soul and identity and the boundaries between the natural and artificial with emerging societal issues related to AI, including gender, racism, and slavery.

title: CLA 3891 Crime and Punishment in Ancient Athens 3 Credits
description: Examines how the Athenians defined, regulated, and suppressed crime and what the political discourse on crime reveals about Classical Athens.

title: CLA 3930 Special Topics in Classical Civilization 3 Credits
description: Examines various aspects of Greek and Roman culture based on the ancient sources, literary, and archaeological.

title: CLA 4905 Individual Study 1-4 Credits
description: Reading, conference and reports. All work in will be in translation.

title: CLA 4911 Undergraduate Research in Classical Civilization 0-3 Credits
description: Provides firsthand, supervised research in Classical Civilization. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery or application in Classical Civilization.

title: CLA 4931 Classics Capstone Seminar 3 Credits
description: Introduces scholarly literature on a significant topic in classics. Students will write a research paper showing proper use of primary and secondary sources on the topic.

title: CLA 4956 Overseas Studies 1 1-15 Credits
description: Provides a mechanism by which coursework taken as part of an approved study abroad program can be recorded on the UF transcript and counted toward graduation.

title: CLT 2044 English Vocabulary from Greek and Latin 3 Credits
description: Historical study of the origins, development, and lexicography of English vocabulary with emphasis on elements derived from Greek and Latin.

title: CLT 2374 Classical Mythology 3 Credits
description: Examines selection of myths, or sacred stories, of the Ancient Greeks and Romans, and the numerous ways they used these stories, including through history, literature, philosophy, art and architecture. Explores how aspects of Greek and Roman civilization have contributed to Western culture. Analyzes major works of the Western canon based on these myths and their adaptations.

title: CLT 3123 Survey of Roman Literature 3 Credits
description: Critical study of the development of Roman literature. Includes readings from Plautus, Cicero, Catullus, Vergil, Ovid, and Tacitus. Read in translation.

title: CLT 3291 Greek Drama 3 Credits
description: Classical Greek theater: archaeological remains of important theaters and selected plays of Aeschylus, Sophocles, Euripides, Aristophanes, and Menander. Read in translation.

title: CLT 3340 Ancient Greek and Roman Epic 3 Credits
description: Examines the origin and development of the ancient Greek and Roman epic traditions in the political and social context of the Mediterranean region and Near East from the first millennium BCE through the first century CE.

title: CLT 3371 Religions of the Graeco-Roman World 3 Credits
description: Development, importance, and influence of Greek and Roman religion and cult practice. The main literary and epigraphical sources are read in translation.

title: CLT 3421 Classics and Science Fiction 3 Credits
description: Studies science fiction's origins in Greek and Roman texts to investigate the influence of Greco-Roman literature on modern works of science fiction.

title: CLT 3510 Ancient World in Film 4 Credits
description: Examines film and television characterizations of the ancient world and how these accord with known facts through historical and archaeological evidence. Viewing of selected movies along with background readings, lectures, and discussion.

title: CLT 3531 Legendary Rome 3 Credits
description: Myths and history associated with Rome's early foundation stories and examination of works of literature about Rome's foundation in their political, social, and cultural contexts.

title: CLT 3930 Special Topics in Classical Literature 3 Credits
description: Examines various aspects of Greek and Roman literature with focus on special genres (e.g., historiography), periods (e.g., the age of Augustus), or subjects (e.g., slavery).

title: CLT 4905 Individual Study 1-4 Credits
description: Reading, conference and reports. All works are read in translation.

title: GMT 3513 Greece in the European Context in the 20th Century 3 Credits
description: Overview of Greek politics, society, and culture in the 20th century.

title: GMT 4110 The Literature of Byzantium 3 Credits
description: Overview of major genres of Byzantine literature (Patristic literature, oratory, historiography, chronicle, hagiography, hymnography, epic poetry) from the years CE 330-1453.

title: GMT 4911 Undergraduate Research in Modern Greek Language and Literature 0-3 Credits
description: Provides firsthand, supervised research. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery, or application.

title: GRE 1120 Beginning Ancient Greek 1 4 Credits
description: The basics of ancient Greek grammar, morphology, syntax and vocabulary.

title: GRE 1121 Beginning Ancient Greek 2 4 Credits
description: The basics of ancient Greek grammar, morphology, syntax and translation, with special attention to irregular verbs.

title: GRE 1130 Accelerated Beginning Ancient Greek 1 5 Credits
description: This course and its sequel, GRE 1131, constitute the basic sequence for development of overall skill in the language.

title: GRE 1131 Accelerated Beginning Ancient Greek 2 5 Credits
description: Continuation of the basic sequence for development of overall skill in the language.

title: GRK 1130 Beginning Modern Greek 1 5 Credits
description: This course and its sequel, GRK 1131, constitute the basic sequence for development of overall skill in the language.

title: GRK 1131 Beginning Modern Greek 2 5 Credits
description: Continuation of the basic sequence for development of overall skill in the language.

title: GRK 2200 Intermediate Modern Greek 1 3 Credits
description: Readings in modern Greek literature, history and culture.

title: GRK 2201 Intermediate Modern Greek 2 3 Credits
description: Additional readings in modern Greek literature, history and culture.

title: GRK 4300 Modern Greek Literature Since 1830 3 Credits
description: Advanced study of representative modern Greek prose, poetry and drama in the original from independence (1830) to the present. The course combines the study of the modern Greek language with readings, analysis and discussion of major literary works.

title: GRK 4905 Individual Work in Modern Greek 1-4 Credits
description: For advanced students who seek independent work not offered in another course. Must be arranged individually with Greek faculty.

title: GRW 2211 Intermediate Greek Prose 3 Credits
description: Readings selected from Attic Greek prose authors designed to aid students in the transition from grammar to connected prose passages.

title: GRW 2240 New Testament Greek 3 Credits
description: Reviews grammar and forms. Readings from several books of the New Testament.

title: GRW 3102 Survey of Greek Literature 2 3 Credits
description: Studies representative texts from various periods of Greek literature.

title: GRW 3301 Greek Drama 3 Credits
description: Selected plays of Aeschylus, Sophocles or Euripides.

title: GRW 3501 Plato 3 Credits
description: Studies Plato's Meno and Apology.

title: GRW 4330 Greek Lyric Poetry 3 Credits
description: Translation and analysis of Greek lyric poetry, from Archilochus to Bacchylides.

title: GRW 4340 Homer and Greek Epic 3 Credits
description: Translation and analysis of selections from Homer's Iliad and Odyssey.

title: GRW 4380 Greek Historians 3 Credits
description: Translation and analysis of selections of Herodotus, Thucydides, Xenophon, and Plutarch.

title: GRW 4700 Greek Orators 3 Credits
description: Translation and analysis of selections from Lysias, Demosthenes and Isocrates. (N)

title: GRW 4905 Individual Work 1-4 Credits
description: Reading, conference and reports.

title: GRW 4911 Undergraduate Research in Greek Language and Literature 0-3 Credits
description: Provides firsthand, supervised research. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery, or application.

title: GRW 4930 Studies in Greek Literature 3 Credits
description: Advanced study of a particular author, genre, period or subject.

title: LAT 1101 Beginning Latin 2 3 Credits
description: The second part of the sequence for those with little or no background in Latin.

title: LAT 1104 Beginning Latin 3 3 Credits
description: The third part of the sequence for students with little background in high-school Latin.

title: LAT 1120 Beginning Latin 1 4 Credits
description: The first of a 3-semester sequence for students with little or no background in Latin. Others enrolling in the course will be required to take it for an S/U grade.

title: LAT 1130 Accelerated Beginning Latin 1 5 Credits
description: This course and its sequel, LAT 1131, constitute the basic sequence for development of overall skill in the language. Students are expected students to have little or no background in Latin. Others enrolling in the course will be required to take it for an S/U grade.

title: LAT 1131 Accelerated Beginning Latin 2 5 Credits
description: Continuation of the basic sequence for development of overall skill in the language.

title: LIT 2000 Introduction to Literature 3 Credits
description: In this course, students will be assigned readings representative of a broad range of literary genres and cultures. These readings will cover a variety of literary movements and historical eras. The readings will include selections from the Western canon. Written analysis of literary works may be required. Students will be provided with opportunities to practice critical interpretation.

title: LNW 2321 Introduction to Vergil 3 Credits
description: Readings in Vergil's Eclogues, Georgics and/or the Aeneid, with emphasis on introducing Vergilian style, diction poetic techniques and basic genre differences. Review of Latin grammar and syntax.

title: LNW 2560 Readings in Latin Literature 3 Credits
description: Examines various aspects of Roman life through readings in Latin literature (with a focus on either special subjects, authors, genres or periods) and a review of Latin grammar.

title: LNW 2630 Latin Love Poetry 3 Credits
description: Translation and interpretation of selected poems of Catullus and a thorough review of Latin grammar.

title: LNW 3220 The Ancient Novel 3 Credits
description: Readings from Petronius, Apuleius or the Historia Apollonii Regis Tyri.

title: LNW 3310 Roman Drama 3 Credits
