description: Provides firsthand, supervised research. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery, or application.

title: JPT 4956 Overseas Studies 1 1-15 Credits
description: Provides a mechanism by which coursework taken as part of an approved study abroad program can be recorded on the UF transcript and counted toward graduation.

title: JPT 4957 Overseas Studies 2 1-15 Credits
description: Revolving topics provide a mechanism for coursework taken at a foreign university as part of an approved study abroad program to be transferred to UF. Credits taken will be entered in the student's transcript and may or may not count toward graduation, at the discretion of major's advisors.

title: JPW 3143 Classical Japanese 1 3 Credits
description: Introduction to classical Japanese texts with emphasis on reading comprehension, grammar analysis and translation.

title: JPW 3144 Classical Japanese 2 3 Credits
description: Complex texts in classical Japanese with focus on comprehension, grammar, literature and culture.

title: JPW 4130 Readings in Japanese Literature 3 Credits
description: A fourth-year language course based on literary texts, incorporating advanced reading skills and the analysis of literature in the original. (N)

title: JPW 4131 Japanese Texts and Contexts 3 Credits
description: Complements JPW 4130, Readings in Japanese Literature, and focuses on contemporary issues as encountered in a variety of Japanese media.

title: JPW 4911 Undergraduate Research in Target Language 0-3 Credits
description: Provides firsthand, supervised research in Target Language. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery or application in Target Language.

title: AML 4685 Race and Ethnicity 3 Credits
description: Variable topics examine issues, movements, forms, or themes related to race and ethnicity in American literature. Topics may include Pacific Rim cultures in America, Chicano-Latino literature, the Black Arts Movement, constructing Native America, border-crossing and migration, post-war Jewish fiction, literature and the psychology of prejudice, comparative representations of racial and ethnic experience, representing whiteness, literatures of assimilation and multi-racial identities. Refer to the department website for offered topics.

title: ANT 3241 Anthropology of Religion 3 Credits
description: Cross-cultural survey of beliefs and practices dealing with the supernatural, magic, and religion. Conceptualization of the supernatural. Sacred specialists, their function, and social position. Theories of comparative religion in light of anthropological data.

title: CPO 3403 Politics of the Middle East 3 Credits
description: Focuses on key political and social processes in the modern Middle East. Addresses variable themes in comparative politics such as nationalism, state formation, women and politics, religious resurgence, feminism, and law.

title: CPO 4000 Selected Studies in Comparative Politics 3 Credits
description: Variable topics in comparative politics; precise course content will be announced in advance.

title: CPO 4727 Judaism and Politics 3 Credits
description: Examines how Jews govern themselves (often referred to as communal governance) and how Jews relate to the political system where they live. Explores the development of the Jewish political tradition in the Biblical period, the Middle Ages, the early modern period and the modern era.

title: ENG 4135 National Cinemas 4 Credits
description: Variable topics study of the films of historically important national cinemas, such as American, French, German, Italian, Russian, Japanese.

title: EUH 3033 History of the Holocaust 3 Credits
description: Origins of anti-Semitism in central Europe and the execution of the Holocaust by Nazi Germany. Examines the ideology of the Nazi leaders and the role of the SS, Army, Police, and ordinary citizens in perpetrating genocide.

title: EUH 3670 Jewish History from 711 to 1492 3 Credits
description: Surveys major historical developments in medieval Jewish society under Islam and western Christendom. Examines the interaction of Jews with the majority culture, political structure and economy, as well as changing cultural trends.

title: EUH 3671 Jewish History from 1492-1750 3 Credits
description: Surveys major trends in Jewish society from the f15th to the 18th century, focusing on community structures and interfaith relations.

title: EUH 3672 Modern European Jewish History 3 Credits
description: Major events and themes of the Jews in Europe from 1650-1945.

title: HBR 1130 Beginning Modern Hebrew 1 5 Credits
description: Beginning Hebrew covers four skills: listening, speaking, reading, and writing. For those with no prior exposure to the language.

title: HBR 1131 Beginning Modern Hebrew 2 5 Credits
description: Continues beginning Hebrew, covering four skills: listening, speaking, reading, and writing.

title: HBR 2220 Intermediate Modern Hebrew 1 4 Credits
description: Intermediate Hebrew study covers four skills: listening, speaking, reading, and writing with new vocabulary and grammar.

title: HBR 2221 Intermediate Modern Hebrew 2 4 Credits
description: Continues intermediate Hebrew covering four skills: listening, speaking, reading, and writing.

title: HBR 3410 Advanced Modern Hebrew 1 4 Credits
description: Advanced study of the four skills in Modern Hebrew: listening, speaking, reading, and writing with attention to more complex structures.

title: HBR 3411 Advanced Modern Hebrew 2 4 Credits
description: Continues advanced study of Hebrew language, including listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills, with particular attention to more complex structures. Emphasizes project-based learning through real-world applications of Hebrew, focusing on cultural, professional, and academic contexts.

title: HBR 4905 Individual Work 1-5 Credits
description: Individual work on an approved topic.

title: HBR 4930 Special Topics 3 Credits
description: Proseminar of variable content providing an opportunity for in-depth study of special topics in Israeli literature, history, or culture.

title: HBT 3223 Identity and Dissent in the Hebrew Short Story 3 Credits
description: Traces the tension between the individual and the collective in Zionist/Israeli society over the last 100-plus years as illustrated in Hebrew short fiction.

title: HBT 3233 Israeli History and the Contemporary Novel 3 Credits
description: Studies Israeli history through the lens of the contemporary novel.

title: JST 2841 Women and Politics in Israel 3 Credits
description: Analyzes women's roles both as political agents and as objects of political policies and movements. Examines Israeli female politicians and judges as political actors, and as both subjects and objects of state policy.

title: JST 3845 Israelis and Palestinians 3 Credits
description: Discussion of Israeli-Palestinian interactions with a focus on the way collective identities are shaped by the conflict. Historically outlines development of the conflict from the beginning of the Zionist immigration to Palestine to present day and includes thematic analysis of its sociological dynamics.

title: JST 3930 Special Topics in Jewish Studies 3 Credits
description: Variable topics in Jewish thought, history, literature, and culture as represented in classified Jewish texts, Jewish law, Jewish ethics, folklore, Hasidism, and Holocaust literature.

title: JST 4905 Individual Work in Jewish Studies 1-3 Credits
description: For advanced students who desire supplemental reading or research under guidance.

title: JST 4936 Colloquium in Jewish Studies 3 Credits
description: Colloquium in Jewish studies designed to enhance knowledge of the development and significance of Jewish civilization.

title: JST 4940 Internship in Jewish Studies 1-6 Credits
description: Preapproved internship with Jewish communal, educational, or service institutions. Only 3 credits can count toward a Jewish Studies major or minor.

title: JST 4970 Senior Honors Thesis 1-3 Credits
description: Directed research leading to submission of an honors thesis. Work completed under the supervision of a Jewish studies faculty member. Students can register for one semester (3 credits) or two (1 and 2 credits respectively). Required for magna or summa cum laude designation.

title: LIT 3173 Jewish Literature 3 Credits
description: Variable topics in the Jewish literary experience, from the biblical narrative and classical tales to Yiddish and Hebrew literature, the modern European novel, and American Jewish fiction.

title: MUH 3621 Jewish Art Music in Western Culture 3 Credits
description: Cultural history of western art music inspired by Jewish subjects, Biblical and non-Biblical, composed by both Jewish and non-Jewish composers, and a survey of Jewish performing musicians from the Renaissance to the present. (N)

title: POS 4291 Religion and Politics in the United States 3 Credits
description: Investigates the role of religious institutions, values and communities in contemporary American political life. (WR)

title: REL 2104 Environmental Ethics 3 Credits
description: Explores competing secular and religious views regarding human impacts on and moral responsibilities toward nature and of the key thinkers and social movements in contention over them. (WR)

title: REL 2210 Hebrew Scriptures 3 Credits
description: History, literature, and beliefs of the Israelites from the Biblical text in the light of modern scholarship.

title: REL 2388 Indigenous Religions of the Americas 3 Credits
description: This course introduces the student to the historical and contemporary religious beliefs and practices of the indigenous peoples of the Americas. Explores how these roots are evident throughout western religious traditions today. (H)(WR).

title: REL 2600 Jews, Judaism, and Jewishness 3 Credits
description: Multidisciplinary approach to the Jewish experience from its Biblical origins to modern times.

title: REL 3213 Hebrew Bible as Literature 3 Credits
description: Intensive introduction to the literary study of the Hebrew Bible within the context of ancient Near Eastern literature and history.

title: REL 3291 Gender and the Hebrew Bible 3 Credits
description: Critical examination of the literary representation and historical realities of gender and sexuality in ancient Israel through close readings of selected texts from the Hebrew Bible.

title: REL 3321 Early Judaism and Christianity 3 Credits
description: Examines the Jewish-Christian encounter. The historical interaction between Judaism and Christianity, including how each group symbolized the other, and the practical implications of the representations.

title: REL 3938 Special Topics in Religion 3 Credits
description: Special topics in religion.

title: REL 4092 Ethics, Utopias, and Dystopias 3 Credits
description: Examines relationships between ethics and utopias in literature, religious communities, and millenarian movements.

title: REL 4209 Dead Sea Scrolls and Early Jewish Literature 3 Credits
description: Explores the varieties of literature that arose within Judaism from 250 BCE to 220 CE, including selections from the Dead Sea Scrolls, the OT Pseudepigrapha, Philo, and Josephus.

title: REL 4221 The Pentateuch 3 Credits
description: In-depth study of the Pentateuch (Genesis-Deuteronomy) in light of modern biblical scholarship.

title: REL 4936 Special Topics in Religious Studies 3 Credits
description: Advanced study for those with proper preparation of selected topics involving one or more religious traditions.

title: HBR 1130 Beginning Modern Hebrew 1 5 Credits
description: Beginning Hebrew covers four skills: listening, speaking, reading, and writing. For those with no prior exposure to the language.

title: HBR 1131 Beginning Modern Hebrew 2 5 Credits
description: Continues beginning Hebrew, covering four skills: listening, speaking, reading, and writing.

title: HBR 1300 Yiddish Language and Culture I 4 Credits
description: Focuses on reading, writing, listening, speaking and grammar, with special emphasis on Yiddish history and culture.

title: HBR 1301 Yiddish Language and Culture II 4 Credits
description: Continuation of HBR 1300. Focuses on reading, writing, listening, speaking, and grammar, with special emphasis on Yiddish history and culture.

title: HBR 2220 Intermediate Modern Hebrew 1 4 Credits
description: Intermediate Hebrew study covers four skills: listening, speaking, reading, and writing with new vocabulary and grammar.

title: HBR 2221 Intermediate Modern Hebrew 2 4 Credits
description: Continues intermediate Hebrew covering four skills: listening, speaking, reading, and writing.

title: HBR 3410 Advanced Modern Hebrew 1 4 Credits
description: Advanced study of the four skills in Modern Hebrew: listening, speaking, reading, and writing with attention to more complex structures.

title: HBR 3411 Advanced Modern Hebrew 2 4 Credits
description: Continues advanced study of Hebrew language, including listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills, with particular attention to more complex structures. Emphasizes project-based learning through real-world applications of Hebrew, focusing on cultural, professional, and academic contexts.

title: HBR 3440 Translation in Israeli Media 3 Credits
description: Explores One of the Most Popular and Fast Developing Areas of Study in Communications and Translation Studies Today: Media and Translation. Focuses on Israeli Context. Areas of Study Include Topics From Subtitling and Captioning to Translating Websites and Television Commercials.

title: HBR 4905 Individual Work 1-5 Credits
description: Individual work on an approved topic.

title: HBR 4930 Special Topics 3 Credits
description: Proseminar of variable content providing an opportunity for in-depth study of special topics in Israeli literature, history, or culture.

title: HBR 4956 Overseas Studies 1-15 Credits
description: Provides a mechanism by which coursework taken as part of an approved study abroad program can be recorded on the UF transcript and counted toward graduation.

title: HBT 3100 Introduction to Israeli Culture 3 Credits
description: Presentation and analysis of some key facets of Israeli culture. Topics include Jewish vs. Zionist (and Israeli non-Jewish) identities, Israeli-Palestinian conflict, Israeli-American relations, gender, and function of art in Israeli society.

title: HBT 3115 Harry Potter and the Holocaust 3 Credits
description: Explores trauma, genocide, and, specifically, the Jewish Holocaust through the lens of children’s literature, using historical and literary approaches.

title: HBT 3130 The Merchant of Venice in Translation 3 Credits
description: Highlights the Central European detour that Shakespearean reception (translation, adaptation, performance, publication and critical response) and the play, The Merchant of Venice, underwent in German, Hebrew, and Yiddish, in various locations, media and critical references.

title: HBT 3201 Biblical Love Poetry 3 Credits
description: Reviews the principles and functions of Biblical verse, contextualizes some of the poems, offers various religious and lay interpretations, and teaches various choice verses in the original Biblical Hebrew.

title: HBT 3233 Israeli History and the Contemporary Novel 3 Credits
description: Studies Israeli history through the lens of the contemporary novel.

title: HBT 3235 Holocaust Novels 3 Credits
description: Reviews the historical and political functions of the Novel as a genre; surveys a number of Holocaust Novels that address several Holocaust related topics: Jewish Ghetto, survivors and refugees, the post-Holocaust experience, and the historical memories of Jews and non-Jews.

title: HMW 4200 Readings in Modern Hebrew Literature 1 3 Credits
description: Readings in modern Hebrew novels, short fiction and poetry. While the study of literature is emphasized, some language work is presented to help with reading comprehension. Texts and instruction are in Hebrew.

title: HMW 4201 Readings in Modern Hebrew Literature 2 3 Credits
description: Readings in modern Hebrew texts, including recently published stories and poems. Class is instructed in Hebrew.

title: JST 2841 Women and Politics in Israel 3 Credits
description: Analyzes women's roles both as political agents and as objects of political policies and movements. Examines Israeli female politicians and judges as political actors, and as both subjects and objects of state policy.

title: JST 2930 Special Topics in Jewish Studies 3 Credits
description: Selected topics in the study of Jewish civilization.

title: JST 3821 Israeli Society 3 Credits
description: Introduces major themes in dynamics of contemporary Israeli society. Juxtaposition of the different subjective points of view and motivations of the various actors involved.

title: JST 3845 Israelis and Palestinians 3 Credits
description: Discussion of Israeli-Palestinian interactions with a focus on the way collective identities are shaped by the conflict. Historically outlines development of the conflict from the beginning of the Zionist immigration to Palestine to present day and includes thematic analysis of its sociological dynamics.

title: JST 3930 Special Topics in Jewish Studies 3 Credits
description: Variable topics in Jewish thought, history, literature, and culture as represented in classified Jewish texts, Jewish law, Jewish ethics, folklore, Hasidism, and Holocaust literature.

title: JST 4905 Individual Work in Jewish Studies 1-3 Credits
description: For advanced students who desire supplemental reading or research under guidance.

title: JST 4936 Colloquium in Jewish Studies 3 Credits

title: JST 4940 Internship in Jewish Studies 1-6 Credits
description: Preapproved internship with Jewish communal, educational, or service institutions. Only 3 credits can count toward a Jewish Studies major or minor.

description: Directed research leading to submission of an honors thesis. Work completed under the supervision of a Jewish studies faculty member. Students can register for one semester (3 credits) or two (1 and 2 credits respectively). Required for magna or summa cum laude designation.

title: LIT 3173 Jewish Literature 3 Credits
description: Variable topics in the Jewish literary experience, from the biblical narrative and classical tales to Yiddish and Hebrew literature, the modern European novel, and American Jewish fiction.

title: MUH 3621 Jewish Art Music in Western Culture 3 Credits
description: Cultural history of western art music inspired by Jewish subjects, Biblical and non-Biblical, composed by both Jewish and non-Jewish composers, and a survey of Jewish performing musicians from the Renaissance to the present. (N)

description: Investigates the role of religious institutions, values and communities in contemporary American political life. (WR)

title: REL 2104 Environmental Ethics 3 Credits
description: Explores competing secular and religious views regarding human impacts on and moral responsibilities toward nature and of the key thinkers and social movements in contention over them. (WR)

description: History, literature, and beliefs of the Israelites from the Biblical text in the light of modern scholarship.

title: REL 2388 Indigenous Religions of the Americas 3 Credits
description: This course introduces the student to the historical and contemporary religious beliefs and practices of the indigenous peoples of the Americas. Explores how these roots are evident throughout western religious traditions today. (H)(WR).

description: Multidisciplinary approach to the Jewish experience from its Biblical origins to modern times.

title: REL 3213 Hebrew Bible as Literature 3 Credits
description: Intensive introduction to the literary study of the Hebrew Bible within the context of ancient Near Eastern literature and history.

title: REL 3291 Gender and the Hebrew Bible 3 Credits
description: Critical examination of the literary representation and historical realities of gender and sexuality in ancient Israel through close readings of selected texts from the Hebrew Bible.

title: REL 3321 Early Judaism and Christianity 3 Credits
description: Examines the Jewish-Christian encounter. The historical interaction between Judaism and Christianity, including how each group symbolized the other, and the practical implications of the representations.

title: REL 3938 Special Topics in Religion 3 Credits
description: Special topics in religion.

title: REL 4092 Ethics, Utopias, and Dystopias 3 Credits
description: Examines relationships between ethics and utopias in literature, religious communities, and millenarian movements.

title: REL 4209 Dead Sea Scrolls and Early Jewish Literature 3 Credits
description: Explores the varieties of literature that arose within Judaism from 250 BCE to 220 CE, including selections from the Dead Sea Scrolls, the OT Pseudepigrapha, Philo, and Josephus.

title: REL 4221 The Pentateuch 3 Credits
description: In-depth study of the Pentateuch (Genesis-Deuteronomy) in light of modern biblical scholarship.

title: REL 4936 Special Topics in Religious Studies 3 Credits
description: Advanced study for those with proper preparation of selected topics involving one or more religious traditions.

title: JOU 2040 Writing Mechanics 1 Credit
description: Prepares media writers for professional work, with a focus on mastering the basics of grammar, punctuation and spelling.

title: JOU 2100 Broadcast Writing Bootcamp 1 Credit
description: Explore the differences of writing for the ear vs. writing for the eye. Focuses on storytelling through methodologies traditionally associated with broadcast journalism—which can also be incorporated in digital and social storytelling. Examines story structures and practical experience in writing scripts and sound-based stories.

title: JOU 2380 Future Food 3 Credits
description: Discusses scientific and technological advances in the science of food. How does modern science improve the food we eat? Investigates and reflects on how modern science is used to improve the health of our bodies and our planet by relying on the disciplines of food science, food engineering, and linguistics. Topics include modern technologies such as artificial intelligence and the cognitive science of taste and food language. Represents these themes on an international level with national and regional examples presented for classroom discussions and activities.

title: JOU 2701 Journalism, Justice, and Civic Change 3 Credits
description: Students in this course will learn to identify and analyze how media content reflects, supports and sometimes curbs societal power structures in ways that influence civic life, particularly at the state and local levels. Students will be exposed to relevant mass communication theories such as agenda-setting and framing.

title: JOU 3015 Principles of Journalism 3 Credits
description: Learn about the role and function of journalism in a democratic society, legal and ethical issues in journalism, economic challenges, the changing landscape of the media industry, and some of the most important pieces of American journalism. Also learn about the major principles of journalism and be introduced to techniques used by journalists to cover society.

title: JOU 3101 Reporting 3 Credits
description: Instruction and practice reporting and writing basic news stories. Emphasizes style, clarity, accuracy, and responsibility in handling news.

title: JOU 3109C Multimedia Writing 3 Credits
description: Introduces news and public relations writing and the use of multimedia tools in gathering and disseminating information. Learn how to use multimedia tools for journalism work, interviewing techniques, AP style and the elements that make a story newsworthy. Consists of two hours of lecture and a three-hour lab each week.

title: JOU 3110 Applied Fact Finding 3 Credits
description: Instruction and practice in researching and solving complex news problems. Emphasizes enterprise, documentation and use of multiple sources, using standard reference works and public records.

title: JOU 3121 DataViz and Mapping 3 Credits
description: Covers the foundational skills required for any professional communicator who seeks to tell a visual data story for audiences that rely on cellphones, as well as the basics of geospatial analysis. Emphasizes data evaluation using code-free tools.

title: JOU 3184 Beat Reporting 3 Credits
description: Experience in reporting on a number of beats of significant public interest. Emphasizes responsible coverage of law enforcement, courts, schools, city and county government, health and the environment.

title: JOU 3202 Editing and Coaching 3 Credits
description: Intermediate newsroom editing and coaching, which teaches thinking like an editor from a macro- and micro-perspective to improve critical thinking toward journalism content across platforms. Also, challenges journalists to report accurately, fairly, and more completely.

title: JOU 3213 Design 3 Credits
description: Design of various forms of print media. Emphasizes basic principles of design. Use of typography and photographs together.

title: JOU 3220C Visual Journalism 3 Credits
description: Learn how words and visuals work together to tell a story. Develop the skills to interpret and evaluate photographs, maps, charts, interactive graphics, websites and motion graphics. Learn and apply basic design principles. Gain proficiency in Photoshop, InDesign and other software.

title: JOU 3305 Data Journalism 3 Credits
description: Experience data journalism by using tools such as spreadsheets, databases, and mapping programs to acquire, clean, and analyze data, and by performing basic data visualization. Focuses on using tools to find meaningful patterns in data and to report those patterns accurately.

title: JOU 3346L Multimedia Reporting 3 Credits
description: Learn to use digital tools in reporting for Internet news sites and other online media. Focuses on the tools to gather audio and visual material that helps to tell a journalistic story.

title: JOU 3363 Introduction to Web Apps for Communicators 3 Credits
description: Introduces web markup, coding, and programming for journalism and communications students with no prior coding experience. Explore media-industry best practices for front-end web development, problem solving and algorithmic thinking, and recent examples of interactives and apps from media organizations.

title: JOU 3365 Artificial Intelligence in Media and Society 3 Credits
description: Gain an understanding of artificial intelligence as it applies to the media professions, including journalists reporting on AI. Explore developments in AI technologies as covered by the mass media. Learn to detect exaggeration in descriptions of AI’s promise and potential risks and dangers.

title: JOU 3366C Social Media for Journalists 3 Credits
description: Learn to develop strategy and execute tactics on social media platforms and social media accounts used by news organizations and individuals in professional contexts; write individual posts for various purposes and goals; produce photos, videos, and graphics for social sharing.

title: JOU 3601C Photographic Journalism 3 Credits
description: Study and practice of photography as a major component of journalism. Emphasizes newspaper/magazine style photography and use of photographs; ethical, historical, legal, and stylistic aspects. Digital cameras provided by the department.

title: JOU 3920 Professional Practice 1 Credit
description: Explores both traditional job-seeking methods as well as digital job hunting. Covers best practices in how to prepare for an interview and how to reach out to and search for employers online.

title: JOU 4004 History of Journalism 3 Credits
description: Origin, development, and potentiality of print and broadcast media. Analyzes the evolution of standards, policies, methods, and controls.

title: JOU 4008 Journalism Studies 3 Credits
description: Gain media literacy and become an educated consumer, critical thinker and researcher of/about the media. Learn about research methods, media-business models and the connections between the media and economics.

title: JOU 4011C Audience Engagement 3 Credits
description: The relationship between journalists and their audiences has completely changed from a one-way broadcast and publish model of the past. Journalists need to have a respect for and understanding of the people the journalism is designed to reach and serve. Develop audience engagement strategies, explore evidence-based theories of how information is shared and processed, and develop an audience engagement project for a chosen community or group.

title: JOU 4111 Advanced Reporting 3 Credits
description: Produce quality news and feature stories for publication. Gain experience enhancing your news gathering and writing skills. Produce clips for your portfolio and as possible entries in the Hearst Journalism Awards Program.

title: JOU 4123 Investigative Reporting 3 Credits
description: Learn to develop and execute an investigative reporting project employing various story platforms. Investigate a data-driven topic of public interest over the course of the semester; melding both primary research and in-depth interviewing.

title: JOU 4181 Public Affairs Reporting 3 Credits
description: Instruction and practice in basic public affairs reporting; emphasizes responsible coverage of courts, schools and city and county government.

title: JOU 4201 News Center Practicum 1-3 Credits
description: Creating and editing local news content in the College of Journalism and Communications’ professional news center for broadcast and digital platforms serving north central Florida; emphasizes news judgement that serves the audience.

title: JOU 4202 Advanced News Center Practicum 3 Credits
description: Work in an advanced media setting on multiple projects, including writing, editing, and multimedia work. Emphasizes collaboration and decision-making in a real-world setting.

title: JOU 4214 Advanced Design 3 Credits
description: Advanced design and production of various forms of print media. Emphasizes advanced principles, picture editing, typography, and use of other design elements.

title: JOU 4304 Science Journalism 3 Credits
description: This seminar-style course introduces the art and craft of producing well-written, engaging science news and feature stories for print, online and broadcast media. Students learn how to find science story ideas, interview sources, simplify complex concepts, sharpen their science storytelling skills, write publication-ready stories and pitch to editors.

title: JOU 4306 Advanced Data Journalism 3 Credits
description: Hands-on approach which blends journalism and data science to equip students to be a full-time data journalist. Learn to program in R to replace spreadsheets and databases with reproducible data analysis, and to clearly communicate results to peers and lay audiences.

title: JOU 4308 Magazine and Feature Writing 3 Credits
description: Preparation of features and articles for publication in newspapers and magazines coordinated with study of magazine editing problems. Supervised marketing of pieces produced.

title: JOU 4311 Narrative Nonfiction Writing 3 Credits
description: Advanced writing course in which students create article ideas, research and write magazine-length nonfiction articles and submit them for publication.

title: JOU 4313C Sports Reporting 3 Credits
description: Instruction and practice in reporting sports with special emphasis on game coverage and interviewing techniques. Includes features, sidebars, advances and press conference coverage. Opportunities for publication of stories.

title: JOU 4314 Environmental Journalism 3 Credits
description: Introduces the most accurate, credible, and timeliest information on environmental science and related issues; discerning uncompromised expert sources, using human narratives, and descriptive storytelling to relate real world impact, and tapping the databases, records, and other tools used by environmental reporters.

title: JOU 4318 Sports Data Journalism 3 Credits
description: Helps to think empirically about sports journalism and create original stories via spreadsheets, databases, web scraping, and statistics. Evaluate individual and team performance, assess financials, and test assumptions such as the hot hand. A working knowledge of journalism is required, but no prior data expertise necessary.

title: JOU 4327 Video Storytelling 3 Credits
description: Learn advanced storytelling, shooting, and editing techniques for narrative multimedia journalism. Also learn to recognize and understand the technical and aesthetic aspects of multimedia narratives, and how to build successful visual narratives using a combination of still images, HD video, ambient audio, and natural voices.

title: JOU 4364 Advanced Web Apps for Communicators 3 Credits
description: Add server-side (back-end) web skills to already developed client-side (front-end) web skills to develop web apps that include a server-side component to support presentations of stories/data for media. Also covers web scraping to enable journalists to gather open data.

title: JOU 4447C Applied Magazines 3 Credits
description: Provides experience in editing and publishing a magazine.

title: JOU 4510 Magazine Management and Publication 3 Credits
description: Provides knowledge and training in magazine writing, editing, design, production and administration for magazine sequence majors. Stresses organization, concept, audience, budget, printing, advertising, circulation and promotion of magazines.

title: JOU 4604 Advanced Photographic Journalism 1 3 Credits
description: Primary concern is portfolio-quality single story-telling pictures and multiple picture packages, including picture stories. Introduces computer page layout. Deadline pressure emphasized; overview of picture editor duties; trends; social implications; portfolio preparation. Assignments involve computer programs. Students furnish supplies, most 35mm camera equipment and flash.

title: JOU 4605 Advanced Photographic Journalism 2 3 Credits
description: Continues JOU 4604 with emphasis on the picture story, layouts, and the study of historical figures in photojournalism. Pays particular attention to magazines, color, and new developments in the profession. Duties of the picture editor covered in-depth. Prior knowledge of computer scanning and layout programs expected. Portfolio preparation concluded. Terminal course in the sequence. Students must furnish supplies and most equipment.

title: JOU 4700 Problems and Ethics of Journalism in Society 3 Credits
description: Reading, analysis, and planning in current and projected issues in journalism, both in their professional and societal aspects.

title: JOU 4714 Race, Sports and Media 3 Credits
description: This course examines the relationship of sports and media and how it reinforces social values and sometimes challenges social norms. It also spotlights issues involving sports, race, gender, religion, sex and sexuality; how matters related to race, criminal justice, politics, patriotism, militarism, etc., affect mass perceptions of athletes; and stories of athletes who not only have overcome adversity, but also used their public role to advocate for broader social justice and self-determination.

title: JOU 4905 Individual Problems in Journalism 1-3 Credits
description: Students and the instructor concerned choose a problem or project which gives the student actual experience in his or her major field.

title: JOU 4910 Journalism Undergrad Research 0-3 Credits
description: Provides firsthand, supervised research. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery, or application, depending on the topic. (S/U)

title: JOU 4930 Special Study in Journalism 1-3 Credits
description: Variable content provides opportunity for study in academic areas of journalism, such as the literature of journalism and other fields within the province of the college.

title: JOU 4940 Journalism Internship 1-3 Credits
description: Student and instructor select an appropriate work area related to the field of journalism for on-the-job training. Student works a minimum of 100 hours on the job for every credit to be received. Progress reports and summary required.

title: JOU 4941 Fresh Take Florida 3 Credits
description: Work closely with instructors as professional journalists to cover issues of statewide importance, including upcoming general elections, Florida’s governor, Legislature, Supreme Court and other aspects of public policy and state government. Students will produce in-depth content under their own bylines that is distributed to and published or broadcast by leading news organizations.

title: JOU 4950 Applied Journalism 3 Credits
description: By assuming the role of a professional journalist, prepare for professional-caliber reporting, writing, and production of multimedia storytelling suitable for publication in various traditional/digital outlets. Classes are conducted through a series of seminars and heavily coached group projects.

title: JOU 4951 Noticias 1-3 Credits
description: Focuses on Spanish-language news production; take on roles for WUFT's Noticias, including writer, reporter, sports reporter/anchor, co-anchor/executive producer, producer, associate producer, writer, reporter, sports reporter/anchor, and co-anchor, entertainment reporter/anchor, camera operators, and teleprompter.

title: MMC 1009 Introduction to Media and Communications 1 Credit
description: Introduces the tools, resources, and academic and extra-curricular activities offered by the College of Journalism and Communications. Includes lessons on the history and organization of the college and academic and career preparation.

title: MMC 2121 Writing Fundamentals for Communicators 3 Credits
description: One-third of the course is to ensure students have sufficient skill in grammar and punctuation to write with clarity. In two-thirds of the course, students put principles of good writing into practice with short writing assignments that have real-world applications.

title: MMC 2450 Data Literacy for Communicators 1 Credit
description: Numeracy which enables data literacy; to use, evaluate, and communicate numbers in situations commonly faced in advertising, journalism, public relations, and telecommunications.

title: MMC 2604 Media, Cultures, and Identity 3 Credits
description: Examines the role of media in facilitating, challenging, and enabling power dynamics as well as shaping identity in the United States and globally. Looks at various media forms and constructions of race, gender, sexuality, class, religion, and ability. Analyses the relationship between media, identity and power, as well as individual role as a media consumer.

title: MMC 2613 Journalism, Justice & Civic Change 3 Credits
description: Covers intersection between news media and state/local government. Students will analyze how media interacts with societal power structures in ways that influence social and racial justice. Through analysis of investigative journalism and theories like agenda-setting and framing, students will learn how to use information to become more effective civic participants.

title: MMC 3030 Personal Branding for Communicators 1 Credit
description: Professional development course that stresses how to communicate and connect as professionals. Emphasizes mastery of writing, speaking, presentation and employment-seeking skills, working with media, handling media interviews and using social media to establish a professional identity.

title: MMC 3203 Ethics and Problems in Mass Communications 3 Credits
description: A cross-disciplinary introduction to ethics-relevant situations faced by media professionals. Topics include professional standards of conduct, audience representation and engagement and issues associated with the production, presentation and delivery of messages that reflect the best interests of audiences, clients and stakeholders.

title: MMC 3254 Media Entrepreneurship 1 Credit
description: Introduces media entrepreneurship with a focus on how digital technologies are transforming industries. Work in teams to develop new digital media businesses. Develop and pitch ideas, explore market analysis, develop business and financial plans, and study social media strategies.

title: MMC 3420 Consumer and Audience Analytics 3 Credits
description: Provides practical analytical skill-sets, benefiting those who plan careers in analytics/research, social media, media business, advertising/marketing, and public relations.

title: MMC 3702 Rock 'N Roll and American Society Part 1 3 Credits
description: Studies the role of popular music in American culture. Not a music course but a look at the effects of recorded sound on popular culture. Part 1 emphasizes rock 'n roll and its impact from 1954-1970. Part 2 covers 1970 to the present.

title: MMC 3703 Sports Media and Society 3 Credits
description: Relationships between sports, athletes, media, and audience. Includes the evolution of sports media from the early sportswriters to the day when athletes control their own messages via Twitter. Covers the various mediums (newspapers, magazines, books, radio, TV, online, forums, blogs, and social media) in terms of their history, function, and impact.

title: MMC 3742 Rock 'N' Roll and American Society Part 2 3 Credits
description: Studies the role of popular music in American culture. It is not a music course but a look at the effects of recorded sound on popular culture. Part 1 emphasizes rock 'n' roll and its impact from 1954-1970. Part 2 covers 1970 to 1990.

title: MMC 4200 Law of Mass Communication 3 Credits
description: Understanding the law, which guarantees and protects the privileges and defines the responsibilities of the mass media. Includes problems of constitutional law, libel, privacy, and governmental regulations.

title: PGY 3610 Survey of Photojournalism 2 Credits
description: Not open to photojournalism majors. Introduces still photography; selection and use of photographs in the print media; legal, historical, stylistic, ethical and technical aspects. Picture-taking minimal compared to JOU 3601. Shared cameras provided.

title: RTV 3106 Writing and Reporting for Interactive Media 3 Credits
description: Planning and executing news and online news projects. Course lecture, readings and discussion focus on locating, evaluating and tracking Internet sources, construction of multilinear news content and the integration of Internet technology into the news process.

title: RTV 3303 Audio News and Reporting 3 Credits
description: Basic writing, reporting and production of radio news stories and newscasts. Students must purchase approved recorder for class.

title: RTV 3304 Advanced Audio Storytelling 3 Credits
description: Advanced reporting, writing, and production of radio news stories and newscasts against deadlines. Requires radio news laboratory.

title: RTV 3305 In-Depth Broadcast Reporting 3 Credits
description: Principles of investigative reporting as applied to television and radio news.

title: RTV 3601 Broadcast Performance 3 Credits
description: Development of basic radio and television announcing skills.

title: RTV 3632 Broadcast News Producing 3 Credits
description: Producing news content for television. Includes instruction in AP ENPS software, field producing techniques, and production of long-form programs, with considerable emphasis on the role of the producer as gatekeeper and newsroom manager.

title: RTV 4301 TV News Reporting 3 Credits
description: Basic writing, reporting, and production of television news stories.

title: RTV 4594 Advanced Reporting for Interactive Media 3 Credits
description: Teaches the advanced skills necessary to produce online journalism content utilizing video, audio, photo essays, and interactive graphics. Receive practical experience working in the college's converged newsroom.

title: RTV 4681 Advanced TV News Reporting 3 Credits
description: News reporting, videography, writing, and editing for television newscasts on deadline. One day-long lab in television newsroom required and purchase of additional approved video cassettes may also be required.

title: RTV 4684 Advanced Broadcast News Producing 3 Credits
description: Supervised assignment as line or field producer for radio, TV, or online news program or series. Requires eight hours per week in newsroom.

title: KOR 1130 Beginning Korean 1 5 Credits
description: Beginning study covering speaking, listening, reading, writing, and cultural interaction.

title: KOR 1131 Beginning Korean 2 5 Credits
description: Continued study of speaking, listening, reading, writing and cultural interaction.

title: KOR 2230 Intermediate Korean 1 5 Credits
description: Intermediate study of the four skills with new vocabulary and grammar.

title: KOR 2231 Intermediate Korean 2 5 Credits
description: Continuation of intermediate study of the four skills with new vocabulary and grammar.

title: KOR 4930 Special Topics in Korean Studies 3 Credits
description: Variable topics dealing with specific issues in and in-depth study of special topics in Korean studies.

title: KOT 3500 Introduction to Korean Culture 3 Credits
description: Introduction to Korean culture with emphasis on tracing the origin and development of important aspects of Korean literature, art, religion, and society. Taught in English.

title: LAA 1300C Introduction to Design Communication 1 Credit
description: Drawing conventions including plan, section/elevation, and three-dimensional drawing to create visual communications.

title: LAA 1330 Site Analysis 3 Credits
description: Inventory, analysis and evaluation of site development procedures; emphasis on landscape ecology.

title: LAA 1600 Urban Nature and Society 3 Credits
description: Explores the broad environmental, social, and health benefits that nature and open space provide to our urban environments. From both a historical and modern-day context, highlights foundational parks and infrastructural works found in various cities within the United States.

title: LAA 1700 Cultures of Landscapes in the American South 3 Credits
description: This course will employ an interdisciplinary approach to exploring the history, evolution, and present landscape of southern US culture. It looks at how people live with landscape, how southern authors employ landscapes as settings for narratives, how fruits of the land craft distinct regional cuisines, and how landscapes function as carriers of cultural memory.

title: LAA 1920 Introduction to Landscape Architecture 3 Credits
description: Introduces landscape architecture, a profession defined as an art and science of planning or designing on the land-arranging and creating spaces and objects in a landscape for human use. Open to all students.

title: LAA 2310 Sustainable Landscape Technologies 1 3 Credits
description: Theoretical, cultural, ecological, and practical dimensions of planting and irrigation design.

title: LAA 2360C Landscape Architecture Design 1 4 Credits
description: Focuses on the integration of communication skills with design thinking, emphasizing spatial organization and foundational design principles.

title: LAA 2361C Planting Design Studio 5 Credits
description: The planting design studio introduces the planting design process, its relationship to the site planning, existing site conditions, project locale and client needs.

title: LAA 2365C Landscape Architecture Design 2 4 Credits
description: Understanding and application of landscape architectural design processes, design thinking, spatial organization, and communication skills.

title: LAA 2376C Design Communications 1 3 Credits
description: Exploration of intermediate hand and digital drawing tools and techniques to enhance visual communication and design representation.

title: LAA 2379C Design Communications 2 3 Credits
description: Exploration of digital tools and workflows essential for modern landscape architecture.

title: LAA 2710 History of Landscape Architecture 3 Credits
description: Landscape architecture is the art and science of arranging functions and spaces within the ecology of the land and the culture of the humans who inhabit it. Survey of the history of humans as it is expressed in such diverse areas as urban form, community planning, gardens, parks and recreational areas, agricultural patterns and land management from both western and global contexts.

title: LAA 2951 Landscape Architecture Experience 1 1 Credit
description: Explores Florida and the southeastern United States landscapes through the lens of landscape architecture.

title: LAA 3230 Theories of Landscape Architecture 3 Credits
description: Explores the theories pertinent to the practice and study of landscape architecture. Addresses primarily aesthetic and cultural principles and values with related ecological aspects.

title: LAA 3233 Landscape Architecture Experience 3 1 Credit
description: Immerses students in current topics and innovative practices within landscape architecture, offering a hands-on experience that explores dynamic themes such as placemaking, exhibition design, and art in the garden.

title: LAA 3312 Sustainable Landscape Technologies 3 3 Credits
description: Site layout, materiality, and construction detailing for flatwork (pavements) and vertical construction (walls, columns, and wood construction), with an emphasis on design details.

title: LAA 3350C Landscape Architecture Design 3 5 Credits
description: Application of the design process with a focus on site design and planning principles, and design performance assessment.

title: LAA 3353C Landscape Architecture Design 4 5 Credits
description: Explores the physical, functional, and environmental factors that shape the design and planning of towns, cities, and public spaces to support human activity and experience.

title: LAA 3394C Advanced Design Communication 3 Credits
description: Focuses on advanced-level digital tools and techniques used in landscape architecture.

title: LAA 3420 Sustainable Landscape Technologies 2 3 Credits
description: Introduction to essential technical skills in grading, drainage, and stormwater management for implementing landscape architectural design solutions.

title: LAA 3421 Landscape Construction 2 5 Credits
description: Design and construction drawings for public and private landscape structures, landscape utilities, and irrigation system layouts; specifications, takeoffs and estimating.

title: LAA 3950 Landscape Architecture Experience 2 1 Credit
description: Introduces the iconic works of landscape architecture through a weeklong field experience in an American city. Practice field observation and documentation techniques, engage in team-based learning, and develop an understanding of the breath of opportunities in landscape architecture.

title: LAA 4210 Landscape Architecture Professional Practice 3 Credits
description: Preparation for professional practice and the understanding of the business of landscape architecture in private and public arenas.

title: LAA 4356C Landscape Architecture Design 5 6 Credits
description: Introduction to environmental planning and design for landscape architects, covering regional suitability analysis, site selection, design development, and impact assessment.

title: LAA 4357 Senior Independent Project Seminar 2 Credits
description: Focuses on proposal writing and information gathering to support the senior independent project.

title: LAA 4358 Senior Independent Project 6 Credits
description: This final semester-long project expands personal interests and talents, hones decision-making abilities, and sharpens professional skills through well-rounded, comprehensive completion of individual projects. Project must be approved by the faculty.

title: LAA 4362 Design Communications for Landscape Architects 4 Credits
description: Immersive experience in visualization techniques in landscape architectural design and planning. Covers traditional drawing conventions (plan, section/elevation and perspective) and builds skills in digital communication and visualization methods.

title: LAA 4364C Landscape Architecture Design 6 6 Credits
description: Professional studio-skill development and the resolution of complex landscape challenges.

title: LAA 4450 Sustainable Landscape Technologies 4 3 Credits
description: Landscape architecture design detailing and implementation methods through the development of construction documents.

title: LAA 4532 Landscape Ecology and Management 3 Credits
description: Explores the relationship between planning and design decision-making and landscape management practices which are, in turn, based on the fundamental principles of ecology and landscape ecology.

title: LAA 4905 Special Studies in Landscape Architecture 1-6 Credits
description: Studio investigations of topics in landscape architecture adjusted to individual needs of advanced undergraduate and graduate students.

title: LAA 4935 Gardens of the World 3 Credits
description: Assessment and discussion of works of landscape architecture with emphasis on user evaluation, design trends, and research directions. Open to all majors.

title: LAA 4940 Landscape Architecture Internship 3 Credits
description: Internship in a private/public sector office under the direction of a registered landscape architect; required for graduation.

title: LAA 4941C Extended Internship with Landscape Architect 9 Credits
description: Internship in a private/public sector office under the direction of a registered landscape architect.

title: LIT 2000 Introduction to Literature 3 Credits
description: In this course, students will be assigned readings representative of a broad range of literary genres and cultures. These readings will cover a variety of literary movements and historical eras. The readings will include selections from the Western canon. Written analysis of literary works may be required. Students will be provided with opportunities to practice critical interpretation.

title: HUM 2420 African Humanities 3 Credits
description: Similar in philosophy and purpose to the basic sequence, with content selected from the philosophies, literature, arts, and music of various African countries and regions.

title: HUM 2424 African Cultures and Literatures 3 Credits
description: A culturally based study of folktales, proverbs, drama, poetry and novels; and how these forms are used to portray African arts and ideas.

title: SSA 3730 Language in African Society 3 Credits
description: The role of language in the development of African societies. Language and nation building.

title: SSA 4905 Individual Work 1-5 Credits
description: For those who seek independent work not offered in another course.

title: SSA 4930 Special Topics in African Studies 3 Credits
description: Variable topics dealing with specific issues in African studies.

title: SST 2501 African Elements in the Americas 3 Credits
description: Traces African influence in the Americas from the arrival of Africans on the continent until the present.

title: SST 3500 Africa through Film & Media 4 Credits
description: Explore the African continent through film and media to provide a critical analysis of issues of (mis)/representation of Africans in selected moving images and printed materials both in Africa and in diaspora.

title: SST 4502 African Oral Literature 3 Credits
description: An overview of African oral literature, introduces methodological and theoretical problems, and examines the sociopolitical and cultural relevance of the literature.

title: AKA 1130 Beginning Akan 1 5 Credits
description: Beginning study covering four skills: listening, speaking, reading and writing.

title: AKA 1131 Beginning Akan 2 5 Credits
description: Continued study of the four skills with additional vocabulary and grammar.

title: AKA 2200 Intermediate Akan 1 3 Credits
description: Intermediate study of the four skills with new vocabulary and grammar.

title: AKA 2201 Intermediate Akan 2 3 Credits
description: Continuation of intermediate study.

title: AKA 3410 Advanced Akan 1 3 Credits
description: Advanced study of the four skills with attention to more complex structures.

title: AKA 3411 Advanced Akan 2 3 Credits
description: Continuation of advanced study.

title: AHM 1130 Beginning Amharic 1 5 Credits
description: Beginning study covering four skills: listening, speaking, reading, and writing.

title: AHM 1131 Beginning Amharic 2 5 Credits
description: Continued study of the four skills with additional vocabulary and grammar.

title: AHM 2200 Intermed Amharic 1 3 Credits
description: Intermediate study of the four skills with new vocabulary and grammar.

title: AHM 2201 Intermediate Amharic 2 3 Credits
description: Continuation of intermediate study.

title: AHM 3410 Advanced Amharic 1 3 Credits
description: Advanced study of the four skills with attention to more complex structures.

title: AHM 3411 Advanced Amharic 2 3 Credits
description: Continuation of advanced study.

title: ABT 3130 Arabic Literary Heritage 1 3 Credits
description: Surveys classical Arabic literature in translation. Covers pre-Islamic poetry and early Islamic poetry, Omayyad, Abbasid and Andalusian literatures. All readings are in English.

title: ABT 3500 Arabic Culture 3 Credits
description: Introduction to Arabic culture with special reference to art, literature, religion, and society. Emphasizes Arab contributions to philosophy, medicine, mathematics, and architecture. All readings in English.

title: ABT 3510 Arab World Through Film 3 Credits
description: Explore Arabic culture and social issues primarily through contemporary Arab and international cinema. Analyze the portrayal of Arab communities in film, considering cultural, political, and historical contexts. Examine the impact of globalization on Arab cinema, intercultural exchanges, and the balance between tradition and modernity. Assess Arabic cinema's role in shaping global perceptions and its contribution to the international film industry.

title: ABT 4131 The Qur'an as Literature 3 Credits
description: Approaches the Qur'an from a literary standpoint by examining its history, structure, style, major themes, and impact on Arabic literature, Islamic thought, and Muslim culture.

title: ARA 1130 Beginning Arabic 1 5 Credits
description: Beginning study covering four skills: listening, speaking, reading, and writing.

title: ARA 1131 Beginning Arabic 2 5 Credits
description: Continued study of the four skills with additional vocabulary and grammar.

title: ARA 2220 Intermediate Arabic 1 4 Credits
description: Continuation of the study of standard Arabic language. Develops reading, writing, listening and speaking skills and helps with comprehension of written and audio texts. Students can use their knowledge of the Arabic language to clearly express their personal views in a meaningful and well-structured language.

title: ARA 2221 Intermediate Arabic 2 4 Credits
description: Continues the study of Arabic language at the intermediate level. Emphasizes developing reading comprehension, writing, listening and speaking, and vocabulary and grammar.

title: ARA 3223 Arabic Media I 3 Credits
description: Introduces high-intermediate to low-advanced students to the language of Arabic media, including print, broadcast, and online sources. Emphasizes vocabulary development, text structure, listening and reading comprehension, and oral presentations. Topics include politics, business, culture, and current events across the Arab world. Prepares students for advanced language use in professional and academic settings.

title: ARA 3241 Spoken Arabic 3 Credits
description: Develops listening and conversational skills at an intermediate level of proficiency. Focuses on a middle variety of Arabic known as Educated Spoken Arabic that enables successful communication with educated Arab speakers from virtually any country in the Arab world.

title: ARA 3410 Advanced Arabic 1 3 Credits
description: Advanced study of the four skills with attention to more complex structures.

title: ARA 3411 Advanced Arabic 2 3 Credits
description: Continuation of advanced study.

title: ARA 3510 The Arab Woman 3 Credits
description: Examines the role and status of Arab women in their respective societies; specifically examines the internal dynamic of Arab culture that influences the role of the Arab woman.

title: ARA 4400 Fourth Year Arabic 1 3 Credits
description: Development to an advanced level of speaking, hearing, reading and writing of spoken and mass communication and literary Arabic.

title: ARA 4401 Fourth Year Arabic 2 3 Credits
description: Continuation of ARA 4400. Development of a more advanced level of speaking, hearing, reading and writing of spoken and mass communication and literary Arabic.

title: ARA 4420 Arabic through the Texts 3 Credits
description: For advanced students of Arabic. Teaches the more complex grammar, idiomatic expressions and sophisticated stylistic forms of the language. Required for the Arabic minor.

title: ARA 4822 Arabic Sociolinguistics 3 Credits
description: Focus on the relationship between language and society in the Arab world. An examination of the different varieties of Arabic and the relation between linguistic variation and other social variables, such as ethnicity, religion, urbanization, social class, gender, power and ideology.

title: ARA 4850 Structure of Standard Arabic 3 Credits
description: Describes and analyzes the sound system, word structure, and sentence structure of Arabic.

title: ARA 4905 Individual Study 1-5 Credits
description: For those who seek independent work not offered in another course.

title: ARA 4930 Special Topics 3 Credits
description: One of the core courses in the Middle Eastern languages and cultures major offered through interdisciplinary studies. Also useful for linguistics, religion, Arabic, Hebrew, and Jewish studies.

title: ARA 4956 Overseas Studies 1 1-15 Credits
description: Provides a mechanism by which coursework taken as part of an approved study abroad program can be recorded on the UF transcript and counted toward graduation.

title: CHI 1130 Beginning Chinese 1 5 Credits
description: Beginning study covering four skills: listening, speaking, reading and writing. Materials are designed for learners with no prior exposure to the language. Those with native background or education for four years or more in a Chinese speaking country must take a placement test before enrolling in any Chinese language class.

title: CHI 1131 Beginning Chinese 2 5 Credits
description: Continued study of the four skills with additional vocabulary and grammar.

title: CHI 2230 Intermediate Chinese 1 5 Credits
description: Intermediate study of the four skills with new vocabulary and grammar.

title: CHI 2231 Intermediate Chinese 2 5 Credits
description: Continuation of intermediate study of the four skills with new vocabulary and grammar.

title: CHI 2340 Chinese for Heritage Learners 1 4 Credits
description: For those with significant bilingual speaking and listening backgrounds. Emphasis is on recognition of the characters and writing, and pronunciation and speaking.

title: CHI 2341 Chinese for Heritage Learners 2 4 Credits
description: To consolidate the foundation built in Chinese for Heritage Learners 1, to expand vocabulary and to introduce more complex grammatical structures. Emphasis is on reading and writing. Those who successfully complete CHI 2341 are eligible for CHI 3410.

title: CHI 3403 Chinese Calligraphy 3 Credits
description: Introductory study of the origin, composition, development, variations and aesthetic styles of Chinese characters with laboratory sessions for appreciating and practicing calligraphic skills. (N)

title: CHI 3410 Advanced Chinese 1 3 Credits
description: Advanced study of the four skills with attention to more complex structures.

title: CHI 3411 Advanced Chinese 2 3 Credits
description: Continuation of advanced study of the four skills with attention to more complex structures.

title: CHI 3440 Business Chinese 3 Credits
description: Development of language skills and protocol issues used in Chinese business environments. Acquire vocabulary, phrases, and sentence patterns essential for business transactions and develop oral presentations, business cards, and resumes.

title: CHI 4050 Fourth Year Chinese 1 3 Credits
description: Introduces and analyzes the documentary prose style used in Chinese newspapers and media. By studying a variety of short texts students develop skill in listening, speaking, reading and writing about issues commonly encountered in Chinese newspaper editorials and in television news programs, debate roundtables, or TV documentaries.

title: CHI 4051 Fourth Year Chinese 2 3 Credits
description: Solidifies and improves students' knowledge of advanced Chinese through literature as a continuation of CHI 4050. Emphasizes formulating strategies to learn the difference between written language and spoken language and approaches meaningful writing with an eye toward careful examination of authentic writing samples.

title: CHI 4850 Structure of Chinese 3 Credits
description: Introduction to phonological, grammatical and discourse structures of Mandarin Chinese, with an emphasis on its contrastive aspects with the English language. (N)

title: CHI 4905 Individual Study 1-5 Credits
description: For those who seek independent work not offered in another course. Includes all individual study courses offered by the Chinese section.

title: CHI 4911 Undergraduate Research in Language or Linguistics 0-3 Credits
description: Provides firsthand, supervised research in Language or Linguistics. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery or application in Language or Linguistics.

title: CHI 4930 Special Topics in Chinese Studies 3 Credits
description: Variable topics dealing with specific issues in Chinese studies.

title: CHI 4935 Senior Thesis 3 Credits
description: Select a Chinese faculty member to act as director for an independent research project that culminates in the preparation of an honors thesis.

title: CHI 4940 Internship 1-6 Credits
description: Gain practical experience that enhances classroom learning.

title: CHI 4956 Overseas Studies 1-18 Credits
CHT 3101 Translation Method: Chinese to English 3 Credits
description: Hands-on introduction to the processes, procedures, and cultural and linguistic challenges relevant to translation from Chinese to English. Development of basic translation skills including strategies on word level, sentence level, and text level. Acquisition of lexicon, structure, and style used in various fields (legal, business, medical) and hands-on experience in translating real-life documents.

title: CHT 3110 Chinese Literary Heritage 3 Credits
description: Introduces pre-modern Chinese literature in translation. Topics chosen from classical poetry, short stories, novels, and drama. Emphasizes the interplay between orthodox values and the folk tradition. All readings are in English.

title: CHT 3123 Pre-Modern Chinese Fiction in Translation 3 Credits
description: Pre-modern Chinese narrative from its philosophical and historical origins to the fiction at the turn of the 20th century. Emphasizes the 16th and 17th centuries when Chinese vernacular fiction flourished.

title: CHT 3124 Modern Chinese Fiction in Translation 3 Credits
description: Surveys modern Chinese fiction in translation. Samples are from the early 20th century through the contemporary era and include writers of the early Republic, the P.R.C., and Taiwan. Focuses on fiction as a vehicle for social change. All readings are in English.

title: CHT 3302 Tales of the Strange in Medieval Chinese Literature 3 Credits
description: Introduces traditional Chinese tales of the strange and primary sources that contributed to their development. Also covers cross-cultural literary theories of the fantastic and uncanny; interdisciplinary literary, discourse, and narrative theories; and three common medieval Chinese worldviews.

title: CHT 3391 Chinese Film and Media 3 Credits
description: Examination of Chinese cinema and other forms of media, such as television, music, and print culture in a broad sociopolitical and historical context. An interdisciplinary approach with a diversity of readings and multimedia tools incorporated into discussions.

title: CHT 3500 Chinese Culture 3 Credits
description: Introduction to Chinese culture with emphasis on its philosophy, language, society, art, and people as a whole. All readings are in English.

title: CHT 3511 Chinese Martial Arts Fiction 3 Credits
description: Overview of Chinese martial arts fiction from earliest times to the present day. Discusses the literary pleasure of watching violence, the relationship between violence and the law, gender ambiguity of woman warriors, the imperial and (trans)national order of martial arts narrative, and the moral and physical economy of vengeance.

title: CHT 3513 Taoism and Chinese Culture 3 Credits
description: Introduction to the general history and culture of Taoism in ancient and modern China: its thoughts, belief systems, cultural influences, practices, and rituals.

title: CHT 3515 Bodies, Bodies, Bodies in Premodern China 3 Credits
description: Surveys a selection of classical approaches to the study of the body before analyzing and comparing several common literary, philosophical, medical (TCM), mantic, religious, and political representations of the human body in different eras of premodern Chinese culture. Tensions between traditionalism and modernity are examined in the final weeks of the course. All readings and discussion are in English.

title: CHT 3523 Hong Kong, Taiwan, and the New Global Cinema 3 Credits
description: Overview of Sinophone film in Hong Kong, Taiwan, and the broader sphere of Chinese diaspora. Addresses film history, culture, and aesthetics.

title: CHT 4111 Dream of the Red Chamber 3 Credits
description: Explores the intellectual and social life of traditional China through the 18th century epic novel, Story of the Stone. Also studies interpretive theories of the novel, both Chinese and Western. All readings are in English.

title: CHT 4603 Journey to the West 3 Credits
description: Exploration of traditional Chinese religious culture, cultural history and literacy expression through a 100 chapter novel known as Journey to the West, or Monkey.

title: CHT 4911 Undergraduate Research in English Translation 0-3 Credits
description: Provides firsthand, supervised research in English Translation. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery or application in English Translation.

title: CHW 4120 Classical Chinese 1 3 Credits
description: Introduction to classical Chinese prose with texts drawn mainly from early histories and philosophical writings (500 BC - AD 100). Emphasis on reading comprehension, grammar analysis and translation.

title: CHW 4121 Classical Chinese 2 3 Credits
description: Continuation of CHW 4120 focusing on classical Chinese prose with texts drawn from early historical and philosophical texts to belles-lettres of the medieval era and later periods. Emphasis on reading comprehension, grammar analysis, and translation.

title: CHW 4130 Readings in Chinese Literature 3 Credits
description: Introduces advanced language students to a sampling of Chinese writers. Materials chosen from classic or modern/contemporary Chinese literature rotated across semesters. All readings in Chinese.

title: CHW 4140 Newspaper Chinese 3 Credits
description: Development of ability to understand and translate the documentary prose style used in Chinese newspapers and academic journals. Introduces literary function words and grammar structures, with comparison to the vernacular. Most readings in the simplified character form used in the PRC; all readings in Chinese. Applications for research on modern China.

title: CHW 4911 Undergraduate Research in Target Language 0-3 Credits
description: Provides firsthand, supervised research. Projects may involve inquiry, design, investigation, scholarship, discovery or application.

title: CZE 1130 Introduction to Czech Language and Culture 1 5 Credits
description: CZE 1130 and its sequel, CZE 1131, offer a comprehensive introduction to Czech, using interactive methods to develop competence in speaking, listening, reading, writing and cultural interaction.

title: CZE 1131 Introduction to Czech Language and Culture 2 5 Credits
description: Continuation of series. A comprehensive introduction to Czech, using interactive methods to develop competence in speaking, listening, reading, writing and cultural interaction.

title: CZE 2200 Intermediate Czech 1 3 Credits
description: Builds reading and writing skills while continuing to develop conversational ability and listening comprehension. Using a communicative approach, provides an overview of Czech grammar.

title: CZE 2201 Intermediate Czech 2 3 Credits
description: Continuation of intermediate study. Goal is to further develop speaking, listening, reading and writing skills.

title: CZT 3564 Modern Czech Culture and Society 3 Credits
description: Overview of Czech literature, film, music, pop culture, and visual arts as shaped by the events from 1918 to the present.

title: FRE 1130 Beginning French 1 5 Credits
description: Beginning French 1 and Beginning French 2 constitute the basic sequence in French for the development of overall skill in the language. Open to those with little or no background in French.

title: FRE 1131 Beginning French 2 5 Credits
description: Continuation of the basic sequence in French for the development of overall skill in the language. Open to those with little or no background in French.

title: FRE 1134 Accelerated French Review 5 Credits
description: Provides a rapid review of basic communicative French as preparation for intermediate French. For those with previous French study but insufficient placement scores to move to the 2000 level.

title: FRE 1180 Elementary French: Review and Progress 3 Credits
description: For those who have previous experience in French but who are not yet prepared for advanced elementary work in the language. FRE 1180 confirms overall language skill in preparation for FRE 1131.

title: FRE 2220 Intermediate French 1 4 Credits
description: Devoted to grammar review and composition as well as to the advancement of spoken proficiency, FRE 2220 and its sequel, FRE 2221, develop reading and writing skills in French.

title: FRE 2221 Intermediate French 2 4 Credits
description: Continued grammar review. Emphasizes practice in reading and developing vocabulary. Selected readings in French and Francophone fiction.

title: FRE 2274 Intensive French Abroad 6 Credits
description: An immersion language course integrating the experience, observations and impressions of students living abroad with a French family (site announced annually). Emphasis on development of language proficiency and cultural awareness. Class meets 12 hours a week. Enhances speaking, reading, writing proficiency and the ability to communicate with native speakers.

title: FRE 3070 Accelerated Introduction to French 5 Credits
description: Accelerated introduction to French. Assumes no previous knowledge of French. Offers a four-skill introduction to the language for those who have completed intermediate level study in another Romance language.

title: FRE 3224 Applied French 1-5 Credits
description: French-language reading and discussion section designed to accompany and complement areas of diverse content offered in other departments. Readings and discussions are in French to develop specific vocabulary and fluency related to the content of the companion course, as well as to provide an international perspective on the issues of the main course.

title: FRE 3300 Grammar and Composition 3 Credits
description: Systematic examination of French grammar. Practice of writing at several levels (summary of texts, descriptions, compositions). Textual analysis of literary and journalistic materials. First course of major sequence.

title: FRE 3320 Composition and Stylistics 3 Credits
description: Develops advanced writing skills through the stylistic study of literary and journalistic texts. Writing assignments focus on development of a variety of skills, including summaries, literary analyses, argumentative essays, etc. Aspects of French grammar are highlighted along with analytical terms and key vocabulary from texts.

title: FRE 3410 French Conversation and Interaction 3 Credits
description: Develops and refines oral and comprehension skills relating to different domains. New vocabulary ranging from the colloquial to the most refined of discourses enables students to recognize and use words and expressions in the proper context; material enables students to move from discussions about themselves to situations they are likely to encounter in daily life abroad, through interviewing techniques and professional interaction.

title: FRE 3440 Commercial French 3 Credits
description: Introduction to business practices in France with particular emphasis on active use of business vocabulary and salient cultural differences. Primary topics include written business communication, financial institutions, trade, and advertising.

title: FRE 3442 Contemporary French Commerce 3 Credits
description: Continues the acquisition of business language with special attention paid to technical readings, marketing, case studies, and the role of France in the European Union. Emphasizes oral communication skills and contrasting US and French business culture.

title: FRE 3500 France through the Ages 3 Credits
description: A study of France within context of the principal historical events that have formed and transformed the nation state, its mentality and its cultural production. Special attention is given to the significant political, intellectual, religious, social and artistic currents that have marked France and its image from ancient times to the present. (N)

title: FRE 3502 Francophone Cultures 3 Credits
description: A study of the cultures of countries or regions where French is used as the (or one of the) official language or, in a less official capacity, by a segment of the population. Study of some of the historical, conceptual, practical and problematic aspects of Francophonie. Also concentrates on one specific area (the Caribbean or West Africa or Quebec, etc., on a rotating basis). Literary samples are included. (N)

title: FRE 3564 Contemporary French Culture 3 Credits
description: An overview of contemporary France that may include the study of politics, economics, education and the arts as well as ideas of national and ethnic identity and France's place in the EU. (N)

title: FRE 3780L Corrective Phonetics 3 Credits
description: A survey of the units of speech cast in practical terms and organized by classes of sounds with particular emphasis on rhythm, vowels, nasalization, diphthongs and the complex phenomena that occur at word transitions in French. Taught in French, in an audio laboratory, with the instructor as monitor and with a manual designed for individualized instruction.

title: FRE 4411 French Communication 2 Credits
description: Focuses on oral practice with emphasis on oral communication and exchanges. It is designed to improve students’ fluency in French and build/expand on their skills.
